translation: Ta, Seen, Meem
commentary:
طسٓمٓ

These are the mysterious letters that open Surah Al-Qasas, the 28th surah of the Quran. We discussed in the previous surah the conversation about these abbreviated letters and their significance, so we won't revisit that discussion here.

translation: These are the verses of the clear Book
commentary:
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ

Those, in fact, are the miraculous signs of the clear book.

Allah ﷻ immediately establishes the divine nature of what follows. These are not mere historical accounts, but ayat—miraculous signs—from the Book that makes everything clear.

translation: We recite to you from the news of Moses and Pharaoh in truth for a people who believe
commentary:
نَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكَ مِن نَّبَإِ مُوسَىٰ وَفِرْعَوْنَ بِٱلْحَقِّ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ

We are reciting to you from the news of Musa and Fir'awn with truth, for a people who want to believe.

The phrase بِٱلْحَقِّ carries profound meaning here. It means not just "with truth" but "with purpose." Allah ﷻ is telling the Prophet ﷺ from the very beginning: Look, this is about you. There's a reason I'm telling you this story. Everything you're going through, you're going to find solace in this account.

This is the third major account of Musa's (AS) story in the Quran, but it serves a unique purpose. The story of Musa (AS) has elements that parallel both the Makkan and Madinan phases of the Prophet's ﷺ mission. When dealing with Fir'awn and his people (the Egyptians), it's like the Makkan Quran—the Prophet ﷺ dealing with the disbelievers of Quraysh. When dealing with the Bani Isra'il, it's like the Madinan Quran—addressing a Muslim community with its own challenges and some problematic elements within.

The expression لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ is remarkable. This story is being given for the benefit of "a nation that wants to come to faith." Allah ﷻ is saying that anybody out there wanting to believe will find in this surah enough to convince them, enough to want to become Muslim. The eloquence, the beauty, the power of this surah should be sufficient if there's even an inkling in someone to want to come to believe.

translation: Indeed, Pharaoh exalted himself in the land and made its people into factions, oppressing a sector among them, slaughtering their [newborn] sons and keeping their females alive. Indeed, he was of the corrupters
commentary:
إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ عَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَجَعَلَ أَهْلَهَا شِيَعًا يَسْتَضْعِفُ طَآئِفَةً مِّنْهُمْ يُذَبِّحُ أَبْنَآءَهُمْ وَيَسْتَحْىِۦ نِسَآءَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ

No doubt, Fir'awn was very high and arrogant in the land, and he made its citizens into factions. He would make a group among them weak, slaughtering their sons and letting their women live. He was no doubt from those who created great corruption.

This ayah presents a powerful opening scene, like the voice-over in an epic film. Imagine footage showing massive armies, oppressive soldiers with weapons, and civilians being pushed around—mothers and children facing killing and murder.

The word عَلَا captures Fir'awn's arrogance and power in the land. وَجَعَلَ أَهْلَهَا شِيَعًا—he divided its people into factions. The word شِيَعًا refers to groups unified by ideology, people who see themselves as different from others. He created a class society, making sure people didn't see themselves as one unified nation.

This is fundamental to his strategy of oppression. If people unify, they become strong and may overthrow him. But if you keep people divided, you can pick them off individually. It's like a bundle of sticks—individually they're easy to break, but together they're unbreakable. Fir'awn understood this political reality, so he kept the Egyptians separate from the Israelites, maintaining distinct classes that wouldn't mix with one another.

The phrase يَسْتَضْعِفُ طَآئِفَةً مِّنْهُمْ describes how he systematically weakened a group among them. When a group is weak, they can't fight for their rights or have control in the land. An oppressor can slowly take away civil rights, one at a time. When they don't resist, he takes away more rights, then more, until eventually even human rights are stripped away.

يُذَبِّحُ أَبْنَآءَهُمْ—he was slaughtering their children. The present tense form indicates this was a regular, cyclical practice, not a one-time event. Imagine people living in such a state that children are being killed before their eyes and they don't even have an uprising. How utterly weak they must have become, reduced to only being able to cry as their own children are slaughtered.

وَيَسْتَحْىِۦ نِسَآءَهُمْ—he let their women live. Some scholars note this actually represents further humiliation beyond the killing of sons. He was eliminating young men—the core of any potential army or civilian uprising. If the population of young men dwindles, there can be no effective resistance.

إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ—He was from those who created corruption. This summarizes his entire regime of oppression and division.

translation: And We wanted to confer favor upon those who were oppressed in the land and make them leaders and make them inheritors
commentary:
وَنُرِيدُ أَن نَّمُنَّ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتُضْعِفُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَنَجْعَلَهُمْ أَئِمَّةً وَنَجْعَلَهُمُ ٱلْوَٰرِثِينَ

And We want to favor those who were weakened in the land, and make them leaders, and make them inheritors.

Notice the shift to present tense: وَنُرِيدُ—"We want," not "We wanted." This is remarkable because the story being told is in the past, but Allah ﷻ describes His plan in the present tense. This is the divine voice-over explaining the purpose behind what the audience is witnessing.

The phrase نَّمُنَّ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتُضْعِفُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ is powerful. The preposition فِى can modify either "those who were weakened" or "favor." So it could mean "We want to favor in the land those who were weakened," or "We want to favor those who were weakened in the land." This dual meaning suggests both their current state of weakness in the land and Allah's intention to favor them with power in that same land.

وَنَجْعَلَهُمْ أَئِمَّةً—We will make them unified leaders. This is significant because Fir'awn divided them, but Allah ﷻ will unify them under leadership.

وَنَجْعَلَهُمُ ٱلْوَٰرِثِينَ—We will make them inheritors. Inheritance only happens when the previous owner is gone. Allah ﷻ is already declaring that Fir'awn will be removed from power, though at this point in the visual narrative, he seems invincible.

For the audience hearing this—whether the Prophet ﷺ or the Quraysh—the question immediately arises: How? How will these utterly broken people, whose children are being slaughtered without resistance, ever gain power against this mighty king?

translation: And establish them in the land and show Pharaoh and [his minister] Haman and their soldiers through them that which they had feared
commentary:
وَنُمَكِّنَ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَنُرِىَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَهَـٰمَـٰنَ وَجُنُودَهُمَا مِنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَحْذَرُونَ

And We want to give them authority in the land, and show Fir'awn and Haman and their armies from among them what they used to fear.

وَنُمَكِّنَ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ—the word تَمْكِين means to establish someone somewhere with authority and charge. They will have real power in the land.

وَنُرِىَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَهَـٰمَـٰنَ وَجُنُودَهُمَا مِنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَحْذَرُونَ—We want to show Fir'awn and Haman and their armies the very thing they feared. Deep down, despite all his oppression, Fir'awn was afraid of these people. That's why he kept them weak. He knew that if they realized their power and unified, they could overthrow him. This huge enslaved population was like a volatile chemical that had to be handled with care and kept separated.

This is serious political reality—he was terrified of an uprising. That's why he targeted the boys, because young men form the core of any army or civilian resistance. If their population of young men dwindles, there can be no effective challenge to his power.

The mention of هَـٰمَـٰنَ here is significant. Haman is not mentioned in Jewish tradition in connection with Musa (AS). For centuries, Western scholars pointed to this as an alleged mistake in the Quran, claiming the name was confused with a figure from a different era. However, when Egyptian archaeological records were deciphered in the late 1800s, researchers found the name Haman listed among Fir'awn's officials with the title "Chief of the Workers in Stone Quarries"—exactly the role described later in this surah when Fir'awn commands Haman to build a tower.

The Quran declares: نَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكَ مِن نَّبَإِ—We narrate to you from the news, the accurate historical account. This is Allah ﷻ defending His revelation through history itself.

translation: And We inspired to the mother of Moses, "Suckle him; but when you fear for him, cast him into the river and do not fear and do not grieve. Indeed, We will return him to you and will make him [one] of the messengers
commentary:
وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنْ أَرْضِعِيهِ ۖ فَإِذَا خِفْتِ عَلَيْهِ فَأَلْقِيهِ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ وَلَا تَخَافِى وَلَا تَحْزَنِىٓ ۖ إِنَّا رَآدُّوهُ إِلَيْكِ وَجَاعِلُوهُ مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

And We revealed to the mother of Musa: "Feed him. Then when you fear for him, cast him into the river. Don't be afraid and don't worry. We will return him to you and make him from the messengers."

After the epic opening describing armies and oppression, the scene suddenly shifts completely. It's as if the screen goes black and text appears: "Meanwhile, in an ordinary household..." The camera focuses on something seemingly insignificant: a mother and her crying baby.

وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّ مُوسَىٰٓ—We revealed to the mother of Musa. This is a special form of revelation, not the prophetic revelation given to messengers, but الإلهام—a divine inspiration placed directly in the heart of a righteous person.

أَنْ أَرْضِعِيهِ—feed him. Imagine the scene: soldiers are going door to door, listening for the sound of babies. Her child is crying loudly. If they hear him, they will kill him. She tries to quiet him with a blanket, holding her hand over his mouth, but nothing works. The soldiers are getting closer. In that emergency moment, Allah ﷻ inspires her heart: Feed him. When she feeds him, he stops crying. The soldiers pass by without even knocking.

But then they circle back for a second sweep. Now feeding alone won't be enough. فَإِذَا خِفْتِ عَلَيْهِ فَأَلْقِيهِ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ—when you truly fear for him, throw him in the river.

Consider what Allah ﷻ is asking. For a mother to throw her child into a river—a river full of dangers, possibly crocodiles—this is unthinkable. Even if her husband commanded it, a mother couldn't do it. Only divine intervention, only revelation from Allah ﷻ, could convince a mother to take such an action. This shows us that special forms of revelation can come to the righteous, not just to prophets.

But the command doesn't end there. After she puts him in the water, won't she be terrified? Won't her eyes keep darting to the river? If soldiers walk in and ask if she has a child, even if she says no, her eyes will betray her. They'll follow her gaze to the water and find him. So Allah ﷻ adds: وَلَا تَخَافِى وَلَا تَحْزَنِىٓ—don't be afraid and don't worry. 

Only Allah ﷻ can command the heart at such moments. Only He can remove fear and grief when they would naturally overwhelm a person. This is beyond human capability.

إِنَّا رَآدُّوهُ إِلَيْكِ—We will definitely return him to you. This promise is emphatic. Allah ﷻ doesn't tell her where the baby will go or what will happen to him, because those details would only cause more anxiety. He gives her exactly the information she needs: your child will come back to you.

وَجَاعِلُوهُ مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ—and We will make him from the messengers. The word مُرْسَلِين has two meanings: those who are sent as messengers (prophets), and those who are delivered safely to their destination. In her heart, she understood he would be delivered safely, though she might not have fully grasped that he would become a prophet.

If she later tells her neighbors, "I had to put my baby in the river, but don't worry, he'll come back to me," what would they think? They'd assume she'd lost her mind from trauma, creating a fiction to cope with losing her child. But this parallels another story: Ya'qub (AS) lost Yusuf (AS) and for years insisted he would return, while everyone called him crazy. A father lost a baby then; a mother loses one now. That one was thrown in a well; this one in a river. That one ended up in a minister's house; this one will end up in a palace. The patterns of Allah's ﷻ plans echo through history.

translation: And the family of Pharaoh picked him up [out of the river] so that he would become to them an enemy and a [cause of] grief. Indeed, Pharaoh and Haman and their soldiers were deliberate sinners
commentary:
فَٱلْتَقَطَهُۥٓ ءَالُ فِرْعَوْنَ لِيَكُونَ لَهُمْ عَدُوًّا وَحَزَنًا ۗ إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ وَهَـٰمَـٰنَ وَجُنُودَهُمَا كَانُوا۟ خَـٰطِـِٔينَ

Then the family of Fir'awn picked him up, so that he could become an enemy and source of grief for them. Indeed, Fir'awn and Haman and their soldiers were all making a grave mistake.

The camera now follows Musa (AS) along the river. Instead of showing us the journey, Allah ﷻ cuts directly to the destination. The scene shifts dramatically—we've left the urban areas where villagers wash clothes by the riverbank. The river has served as the highway, and we've arrived at the presidential palace with its beautiful riverfront, servants, and courtyards.

فَٱلْتَقَطَهُۥٓ—they picked him up. The word الْتَقَطَ means to pick something up that you didn't expect to find, like finding a treasure on the road. Perhaps there's a basket floating, and someone says, "Get that for me, I'm curious what it is."

The divine irony is devastating: لِيَكُونَ لَهُمْ عَدُوًّا وَحَزَنًا—so that he could become for them an enemy and a source of grief. Only Allah ﷻ knows at this moment that this innocent baby will grow up to be Fir'awn's greatest enemy and source of sorrow. The very thing Fir'awn feared and tried to prevent by killing all the baby boys—he himself rescues from the river and brings into his own home.

إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ وَهَـٰمَـٰنَ وَجُنُودَهُمَا كَانُوا۟ خَـٰطِـِٔينَ—Certainly, Fir'awn and Haman and their armies were making a huge mistake. The word خَاطِئِينَ indicates a grave error in judgment. They thought they could prevent Allah's ﷻ plan by killing children, but they themselves became the instruments of that plan's fulfillment.

translation: And the wife of Pharaoh said, "[He will be] a comfort of the eye for me and for you. Do not kill him; perhaps he may benefit us, or we may adopt him as a son." And they perceived not
commentary:
وَقَالَتِ ٱمْرَأَتُ فِرْعَوْنَ قُرَّتُ عَيْنٍ لِّى وَلَكَ ۖ لَا تَقْتُلُوهُ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَنفَعَنَآ أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُۥ وَلَدًا وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ

And the wife of Fir'awn said: "A comfort of the eye for me and for you. Don't kill him. Perhaps he will benefit us, or we can take him as a son." And they had no idea.

The baby has certain features—a skin tone—that immediately identifies him as an Israelite child. The soldiers know he should be killed according to Fir'awn's decree. But before they can act, the wife of Fir'awn intervenes.

وَقَالَتِ ٱمْرَأَتُ فِرْعَوْنَ—the wife of Fir'awn said. This woman, whom we know from other passages to be Asiyah (RA), one of the greatest women in all of human history, sees this child and something extraordinary happens.

قُرَّتُ عَيْنٍ لِّى وَلَكَ—a coolness of the eyes for me and for you. The expression قُرَّةُ عَيْنٍ means something that brings such joy and comfort that your eyes can't look away, something that removes all sadness. She's telling Fir'awn: this child brings me happiness in a way nothing else does.

Does Fir'awn know his wife is depressed? Yes. He's given her a palace, power, everything material—but she remains unhappy. Now, for the first time, he sees her truly joyful. When we read earlier in Surah Ta-Ha: وَأَلْقَيْتُ عَلَيْكَ مَحَبَّةً مِّنِّي—"And I cast upon you a love from Me"—we see it manifested here. Allah ﷻ placed a special love in people's hearts when they saw Musa (AS), even as an infant.

She's so protective of this child that she becomes bold, even though she fears Fir'awn. She goes directly to him: لَا تَقْتُلُوهُ —don't kill him. Notice how she addresses this: she doesn't say "don't kill him for me," but rather عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَنفَعَنَآ—perhaps he will benefit us. She makes it about practical benefit, not just emotional attachment.

أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُۥ وَلَدًا—or we can adopt him as a son. She presents this as a reasonable option.

Notice the separation in her words: قُرَّتُ عَيْنٍ لِّى وَلَكَ—for me and for you. She distinguishes between herself and him, not saying "for us" as a unified couple. There's a distance between them, yet she looks at this child and says, "Even for you, look how beautiful he is."

And incredibly, Fir'awn looks at the child and that special love from Allah ﷻ touches even his heart. This man who has killed thousands of babies, this humanitarian nightmare, looks at Musa (AS) as an infant and says, "Don't kill him."

The parallel to Yusuf (AS) is striking. The minister Aziz said to his wife about Yusuf (AS): عَسَىٰ أَن يَنفَعَنَا أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُ وَلَدًا—perhaps he will benefit us or we can take him as a son. The exact same words, generations apart, in Egypt, about another special child brought by Allah's ﷻ plan into a powerful household.

وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ—and they had no idea. They had no idea what they were really doing, no idea of the divine plan unfolding through their own decisions. This thought, this protective instinct, came from Allah ﷻ, placed in their hearts without their awareness.

translation: And the heart of Moses' mother became empty [of all else]. She was about to disclose [the matter concerning] him had We not bound fast her heart that she would be of the believers
commentary:
وَأَصْبَحَ فُؤَادُ أُمِّ مُوسَىٰ فَـٰرِغًا ۖ إِن كَادَتْ لَتُبْدِى بِهِۦ لَوْلَآ أَن رَّبَطْنَا عَلَىٰ قَلْبِهَا لِتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

And the heart of the mother of Musa became empty. She almost gave away his secret, had We not tied up her heart, so that she could be from the true believers.

While one woman—the wife of Fir'awn—experiences unexpected joy with this child, the camera takes us back to another woman experiencing devastating loss.

وَأَصْبَحَ فُؤَادُ أُمِّ مُوسَىٰ فَـٰرِغًا—the heart of Musa's mother became empty. The word فَارِغًا suggests something completely drained, emptied out, as if someone had gutted her and removed everything from her heart. People would look at her and she appeared paralyzed, like someone in severe trauma who stares without blinking, completely lost.

إِن كَادَتْ لَتُبْدِى بِهِۦ—she almost gave up his secret. She almost looked toward the river when the soldiers came. She almost cried out "My baby! My child!" She nearly jumped into the river herself. The intensity of her emotional state made it almost impossible to maintain the secret.

لَوْلَآ أَن رَّبَطْنَا عَلَىٰ قَلْبِهَا—had We not tied up her heart. The metaphor of tying suggests that if her heart hadn't been bound, she would have run to the river. Allah ﷻ had to intervene directly to prevent her from acting on her overwhelming emotions. Notice the transition in the same ayah—she was overwhelmed, but Allah ﷻ calmed her down, transforming فُؤَاد (the inflamed heart) into قَلْب (the calm, steady heart).

لِتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ—so that she could be from the true believers. This is profound. She was already a believer, but Allah ﷻ says He put her through this trauma so she could become from the truest of believers, from those with the strongest iman.

Why was this necessary? Because Musa (AS) would need two mothers to raise him properly in the palace—two mothers with the strongest possible iman. He would need that spiritual foundation built into him from the beginning, so that whenever he faced trouble, his first instinct would be to turn to Allah ﷻ immediately. He wouldn't develop that reflex unless he had special training. Without a father figure at home who could provide that, he would need to be compensated with two mothers—and both would have to be incredible believers.

This is all part of the divine parenting strategy for Musa (AS). The trials his mother endured weren't just random suffering—they were preparation for the role she would play in raising a prophet.

وَقَالَتْ لِأُخْتِهِ قُصِّيهِ—And she said to his sister: "Follow him closely." The mother doesn't give elaborate instructions. She doesn't say, "I put the baby in the river. You need to follow him. Make sure nobody sees you. Be careful. Watch where you step. Stay in the shadows." She simply says قُصِّيهِ—trace his footsteps, follow him closely, stay right behind him.

There's wisdom in brevity here. The Arabs say: أَرْسِلِ الْحَكِيمَ وَلَا تُوصِهِ—send a wise person to do your job and don't give them too many instructions. When you pick a smart, capable person for a task, giving them excessive instructions actually insults their intelligence. It suggests you don't trust their judgment.

This mother knows her daughter is exceptionally intelligent. She trusts her daughter's mind and judgment completely. So she gives her the mission in just one word: قُصِّيهِ. And we're about to see just how brilliantly this young girl handles herself, demonstrating the wisdom of her mother's trust.

Already in this surah, we've met two women with incredibly strong hearts—two true believers. Now we're about to meet two women with incredibly strong minds—two brilliant, quick-thinking personalities who will play crucial roles in Allah's ﷻ plan.

translation: And she said to his sister, "Follow him"; so she watched him from a distance while they perceived not
commentary:
وَقَالَتْ لِأُخْتِهِۦ قُصِّيهِ ۖ فَبَصُرَتْ بِهِۦ عَن جُنُبٍ وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ

After sending baby Musa (AS) into the river, his mother instructed his sister to follow the basket. The word قُصِّيهِ means to track or follow closely. But notice the intelligence in how she carried out this task. The phrase فَبَصُرَتْ بِهِۦ عَن جُنُبٍ reveals she observed from a distance, from the side. The word جُنُبٍ indicates being at a distance, staying away.

She didn't walk directly toward the basket or stare at it obviously. Instead, she would skip along, draw attention to herself, then glance quickly from her peripheral vision before continuing on. She was strategic, ensuring no one noticed what she was looking at. Even when she looked, she didn't look directly - she kept an eye on the basket from the corner of her vision.

This required remarkable skill. The castle wasn't just a few houses away from their home. She maintained this careful pursuit all the way until she reached the castle grounds, as far as the fence would allow. From there, she could see through and observe what was happening, though she couldn't enter the royal property itself. She stayed nearby, waiting to hear any news. وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ - they had no idea. To anyone watching, she was just a child playing around, nothing suspicious.

translation: And We had prevented from him [all] wet nurses before, so she said, "Shall I direct you to a household that will be responsible for him for you while they are to him [for his upbringing] sincere
commentary:
۞ وَحَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِ ٱلْمَرَاضِعَ مِن قَبْلُ فَقَالَتْ هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَهْلِ بَيْتٍ يَكْفُلُونَهُۥ لَكُمْ وَهُمْ لَهُۥ نَـٰصِحُونَ

Allah ﷻ reveals another element of His divine planning: وَحَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِ ٱلْمَرَاضِعَ - We had forbidden wet nurses for him from before. The word حَرَّمْنَا here doesn't mean religiously prohibited, but rather that the baby would not accept any milk except his mother's. He refused every wet nurse they brought.

This reveals the wisdom behind Allah's command for the mother to nurse him before placing him in the river. The baby had been programmed to recognize only his mother's milk. This is a form of revelation - not prophetic revelation, but instinctive knowledge placed directly into the nature of an infant. The baby couldn't even see clearly yet, but he knew his mother's milk.

The baby's crying became so loud that even his sister outside the palace could hear. The palace staff had to venture outside to search for wet nurses, and that's when the sister saw her opportunity. She approached them and asked, هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ - "Should I direct you?" Notice her careful wording. She didn't say "his family" or "his mother." She said أَهْلِ بَيْتٍ - "a household." She presented it as if she happened to know of a family in the neighborhood, good babysitters who could help.

The phrase يَكْفُلُونَهُۥ لَكُمْ means they would take care of him for you, and وَهُمْ لَهُۥ نَـٰصِحُونَ - they would be sincere toward him. The use of the plural form suggests more than just the mother, indicating it was known to be a household with multiple caregivers. She crafted her words brilliantly, getting close to the truth without revealing the connection.

The desperate palace staff accepted and followed her to the house. When they arrived and the baby accepted this woman's milk immediately, she was hired on the spot. Eventually, those who accompanied the sister figured out this was the baby's actual family - the resemblance, the way the mother looked at the child, everything made it clear. But they kept quiet.

translation: So We restored him to his mother that she might be content and not grieve and that she would know that the promise of Allah is true. But most of the people do not know
commentary:
فَرَدَدْنَـٰهُ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّهِۦ كَىْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُهَا وَلَا تَحْزَنَ وَلِتَعْلَمَ أَنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

فَرَدَدْنَـٰهُ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّهِۦ - So We returned him to his mother. Allah ﷻ reminds us: didn't We tell you We would return him? The entire plan came together through a simple command - feed your child. If she hadn't nursed him, he would never have been programmed to cry for that milk, and this intricate plan would never have unfolded. You want your baby back? Trust Me and feed him. That's all you need to know.

كَىْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُهَا - so her eyes could be cooled, so she could remain calm and keep a close watch on her son. Now two women in this surah have their eyes cooled: Musa's mother and his adoptive mother, the wife of Fir'awn.

وَلَا تَحْزَنَ - and so she would not grieve. وَلِتَعْلَمَ أَنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ - and that she would know with certainty that Allah's promise is true. This wasn't just belief anymore - she experienced it. She lived it. The child she feared would be killed by soldiers was now safer than she could have ever arranged on her own, living in the very palace of Fir'awn, with her as his caregiver, receiving royal protection.

This traumatic experience transformed her into a true believer, and this iman became contagious. Musa (AS) grew up in an environment of iman - his intelligent older sister, his mother with her deep faith, combined with the formal education he received in the palace. This was the spiritual and intellectual foundation that would shape a prophet.

وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ - but most of them don't know. Most people don't know that Allah's promise is true. Most people don't realize how things will work out in the end. But this phrase also indicates that while most people in the palace didn't know she was his biological mother, some did - particularly those staff members who went with the sister to bring her. They saw the truth but remained silent.

translation: And when he attained his full strength and was [mentally] mature, We bestowed upon him judgement and knowledge. And thus do We reward the doers of good
commentary:
وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ وَٱسْتَوَىٰٓ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ - when he reached his mature age. This is the exact language used for Yusuf (AS), and just as that prophet was tested immediately after reaching maturity, Musa (AS) is about to face his trial.

وَٱسْتَوَىٰٓ - and became upright, firm, established. This word indicates he became extremely mature both intellectually and physically. The root meaning suggests a plant that was once a weak twig you could break, but grew into a strong tree that cannot be knocked over. Musa (AS) developed exceptional physical strength and excellent coordination - he could handle himself in any situation.

ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا - We gave him wisdom and knowledge. Allah ﷻ mentions this combination of intellectual and physical strength deliberately. Unlike Yusuf (AS), who received his early education from his father Ya'qub (AS) and then largely self-educated through observation, Musa (AS) had access to the highest forms of formal education in Fir'awn's palace.

Yet both stories teach us the same lesson: whether you have basic education or the most advanced training, good character doesn't require the highest degrees. It requires the right upbringing. Ya'qub (AS) gave Yusuf (AS) a sound early education that served him when he was alone and tested. Similarly, Musa's mother and sister provided him with a spiritual foundation that would guide him through trials, regardless of his palace education.

وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ - and that is how We compensate those who excel. These are the exact words used for Yusuf (AS), connecting these two stories and reminding us that righteousness and excellence in character are rewarded by Allah ﷻ, regardless of one's educational background.

translation: And he entered the city at a time of inattention by its people and found therein two men fighting: one from his faction and one from among his enemy. And the one from his faction called for help to him against the one from his enemy, so Moses struck him and [unintentionally] killed him. [Moses] said, "This is from the work of Satan. Indeed, he is a manifest, misleading enemy
commentary:
وَدَخَلَ ٱلْمَدِينَةَ عَلَىٰ حِينِ غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ أَهْلِهَا فَوَجَدَ فِيهَا رَجُلَيْنِ يَقْتَتِلَانِ هَـٰذَا مِن شِيعَتِهِۦ وَهَـٰذَا مِنْ عَدُوِّهِۦ ۖ فَٱسْتَغَـٰثَهُ ٱلَّذِى مِن شِيعَتِهِۦ عَلَى ٱلَّذِى مِنْ عَدُوِّهِۦ فَوَكَزَهُۥ مُوسَىٰ فَقَضَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ۖ قَالَ هَـٰذَا مِنْ عَمَلِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ عَدُوٌّ مُّضِلٌّ مُّبِينٌ

وَدَخَلَ ٱلْمَدِينَةَ عَلَىٰ حِينِ غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ أَهْلِهَا - and he entered the city at a time of unawareness from its people. This was the afternoon siesta, the low-traffic time when the streets were empty. The city was the economic capital where both the Egyptian elite and the Israelite laborers worked, located away from the palace in the suburbs.

Musa (AS) deliberately chose this time for his visits. He didn't go at night, when evil typically occurs in cities. He went in the afternoon when the Egyptians were resting, so he could discreetly help Israelite families without drawing attention to the fact that someone from the palace was assisting the enslaved people. He wanted to see the real conditions of the society, the struggles of the oppressed - he wasn't content to remain in the comfort of the palace.

فَوَجَدَ فِيهَا رَجُلَيْنِ يَقْتَتِلَانِ - he found two men fighting, trying to kill each other. In the entire empty city, he found only two people, and they were engaged in a deadly struggle. هَـٰذَا مِن شِيعَتِهِۦ - this one was from his people, an Israelite. وَهَـٰذَا مِنْ عَدُوِّهِۦ (wa-hadha min 'aduwwihi) - and this one was from his enemies, an Egyptian.

This phrase reveals something crucial: even though Fir'awn had spared Musa (AS) and elevated him, the Egyptian elite still saw him as an outsider. His skin color was different, his ethnicity distinct. The generals and nobles despised seeing a slave walking the palace halls as an equal. They only tolerated him because of Fir'awn's decree, but they looked for any opportunity to be rid of him. The animosity was barely contained beneath the surface.

فَٱسْتَغَـٰثَهُ ٱلَّذِى مِن شِيعَتِهِۦ - the one from his people called to him for help. اسْتَغَاثَ is like rain arriving at the critical moment when crops will die without it - help arriving exactly when desperately needed, not more than necessary, not less, but precisely what's required in a life-or-death situation.

Musa (AS) saw what he assumed was a typical scene: an Egyptian oppressing an Israelite. This was the normal state of affairs in that society. So he intervened, pushing the Egyptian and telling him to leave the Israelite alone. The Egyptian fought back - a very bad decision, because Musa (AS) was exceptionally strong.

فَوَكَزَهُۥ مُوسَىٰ - Musa struck him with his fist. A single punch to the chest. فَقَضَىٰ عَلَيْهِ - and it ended his case. The man fell dead. Musa (AS) hadn't realized his own strength, or perhaps the man had a weak constitution. Either way, one punch killed him instantly.

قَالَ هَـٰذَا مِنْ عَمَلِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ - he said, "This is from the work of Shaytan." A young, strong man who just knocked someone out might typically feel pride in displaying his strength. Not Musa (AS). His immediate reaction was to recognize the evil in what transpired - not pride, but awareness of Shaytan's influence.

He also realized the broader consequences. When people wake up and find this dead Egyptian, who will they blame? The Israelites collectively. The already-oppressed people would face even worse persecution. Shaytan had created tremendous evil through this incident.

إِنَّهُۥ عَدُوٌّ مُّضِلٌّ مُّبِينٌ - he is an enemy who clearly misleads. Musa (AS) was reflecting: I should have investigated before acting. I should have restrained him rather than striking back. I had the strength to simply hold him. This awareness would shape his response the next time he encountered conflict.

translation: He said, "My Lord, indeed I have wronged myself, so forgive me," and He forgave him. Indeed, He is the Forgiving, the Merciful
commentary:
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِى فَٱغْفِرْ لِى فَغَفَرَ لَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

Because of his maturity - not just physical but spiritual and intellectual - Musa (AS) knew that when a crime is committed, you turn to the Real Judge. قَالَ رَبِّ - he said, "My Master." إِنِّى ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِى - "Indeed I have wronged myself." No excuses, no blaming Shaytan for his own actions. Complete ownership: I am the one who wronged myself.

فَٱغْفِرْ لِى- "So forgive me." Direct, immediate turning to Allah ﷻ in repentance. فَغَفَرَ لَهُۥٓ (fa-ghafara lahu) - then He forgave him. 

But Musa (AS) hadn't yet been to the mountain, hadn't received prophethood, hadn't spoken with Allah ﷻ directly. How did he know he was forgiven? Through inspiration - a form of divine communication that brought peace to his heart. He felt guided about what to do next: don't go to the authorities, that won't solve anything. You need to go into hiding before people wake up and discover what happened.

إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ - He is the Most Forgiving, the Constantly Merciful.

translation: He said, "My Lord, for the favor You bestowed upon me, I will never be an assistant to the criminals
commentary:
قَالَ رَبِّ بِمَآ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَىَّ فَلَنْ أَكُونَ ظَهِيرًا لِّلْمُجْرِمِينَ

قَالَ رَبِّ بِمَآ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَىَّ - he said, "My Master, by the favor You have bestowed upon me." He turned again to Allah ﷻ, recognizing the special favor of forgiveness.

فَلَنْ أَكُونَ ظَهِيرًا لِّلْمُجْرِمِينَ - "I will never be a supporter of criminals." This is profound. Musa (AS) realized that the Israelite who called for help was himself a criminal - the one who ran away from the scene. He had stereotyped the situation: my people are oppressed, therefore anyone fighting them must be evil. But reality is more complex.

This is the Quran's teaching against stereotyping. We shouldn't automatically assume someone is right or wrong based on their ethnicity, nationality or group affiliation. Even in situations of actual systemic oppression, Musa (AS) says: I will investigate, I will not blindly back anyone up just because of their identity.

This lesson is particularly relevant for Muslims, who can be remarkably skilled at stereotyping everyone - by nationality, ethnicity, race, and every other category imaginable. We don't spare anyone. Yet even in a case where there was genuine, systematic oppression of an entire people, Musa (AS) learned: never stereotype, always seek the truth.

translation: And he became inside the city fearful and anticipating [exposure], when suddenly the one who sought his help the previous day cried out to him [once again]. Moses said to him, "Indeed, you are an evident, [persistent] deviator
commentary:
فَأَصْبَحَ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ خَآئِفًا يَتَرَقَّبُ فَإِذَا ٱلَّذِى ٱسْتَنصَرَهُۥ بِٱلْأَمْسِ يَسْتَصْرِخُهُۥ ۚ قَالَ لَهُۥ مُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّكَ لَغَوِىٌّ مُّبِينٌ

فَأَصْبَحَ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ خَآئِفًا يَتَرَقَّبُ - the next morning he was in the city, fearful and watching. Unlike the previous day when he went during the quiet afternoon, this time he had to go in the busy morning. He was sent into the city on some errand, but now he was afraid.

يَتَرَقَّبُ comes from the image of a shepherd holding his goat by the neck while walking through the woods, alert for wolves. It's how you hold your child's hand tightly in a crowded mall. It means constantly watching yourself, looking around - "What was that sound? Who's there?" Every passing policeman makes you pull your hood up and turn down an alley. He was in a constant state of vigilant awareness.

فَإِذَا ٱلَّذِى ٱسْتَنصَرَهُۥ بِٱلْأَمْسِ يَسْتَصْرِخُهُۥ - then suddenly, the very one who sought his help yesterday was calling out to him loudly. Like a scene from a movie, he turned and bumped into exactly the person he was trying to avoid - that same man from yesterday.

يَسْتَصْرِخُ means not just asking for aid, but making a loud scene, almost pretending to need help. And yesterday, when Musa (AS) had helped him, the man carelessly asked his name. Musa (AS), not yet understanding this man's character, gave it. For criminals, even a small piece of information can be lethal.

Now this man was in another fight the very next morning, yelling across the street: "Please, Musa! Remember me? Help me, man!" He was making a scene, drawing exactly the attention Musa (AS) desperately wanted to avoid.

قَالَ لَهُۥ مُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّكَ لَغَوِىٌّ مُّبِينٌ - Musa said to him, "You are clearly a con artist." غَوِيّ is someone who takes advantage of another's confusion, innocence or ignorance. Musa (AS) recognized the pattern: this man was a habitual troublemaker. "Come on, man, I see what happened yesterday. I'm not coming this time."

translation: And when he wanted to strike the one who was an enemy to both of them, he said, "O Moses, do you intend to kill me as you killed someone yesterday? You only want to be a tyrant in the land and do not want to be of the amenders
commentary:
فَلَمَّآ أَنْ أَرَادَ أَن يَبْطِشَ بِٱلَّذِى هُوَ عَدُوٌّ لَّهُمَا قَالَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ أَتُرِيدُ أَن تَقْتُلَنِى كَمَا قَتَلْتَ نَفْسًۢا بِٱلْأَمْسِ ۖ إِن تُرِيدُ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَ جَبَّارًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا تُرِيدُ أَن تَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُصْلِحِينَ

The troublemaker continued fighting the Egyptian on his own. Eventually, the Egyptian gained the upper hand and was about to kill him. Despite his better judgment, Musa (AS) couldn't stand by and watch someone die. فَلَمَّآ أَنْ أَرَادَ أَن يَبْطِشَ - when he intended to forcefully seize the one who was an enemy to both of them.

Notice: Musa (AS) didn't intend to punch this time. بَطَشَ means to forcefully grab and restrain someone. He had learned from yesterday - this time he would only hold the man back, not strike him. He was walking across to restrain the Egyptian.

But the troublemaker misread the situation. He thought: "I can handle this Egyptian, but I know what that guy can do with his fists." Even though Musa (AS) was coming to help him, he panicked and thought Musa (AS) was coming for him.

The only weapon a lowlife has is information, and this criminal had two pieces: he witnessed yesterday's events, and he knew Musa's name. He used both.

قَالَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ - he called out in the middle of the crowded street, "O Musa!" أَتُرِيدُ أَن تَقْتُلَنِى كَمَا قَتَلْتَ نَفْسًۢا بِٱلْأَمْسِ - "Do you want to kill me just like you killed a person yesterday?"

Musa (AS) woke up that morning in hiding, not telling anyone where he was. He was walking over to help this man, and instead the man publicly exposed him in front of everyone. إِن تُرِيدُ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَ جَبَّارًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - "You only want to be a tyrant in the land, pushing people around!"

جَبَّار means a bully, someone who forces their will on others through physical intimidation. The man continued his accusation: وَمَا تُرِيدُ أَن تَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُصْلِحِينَ999 - "And you don't want to be among those who make peace or do good!"

He deliberately drew all attention to Musa (AS) to deflect from his own crime. Now Musa (AS) had to run.

translation: And a man came from the farthest end of the city, running. He said, "O Moses, indeed the eminent ones are conferring over you [intending] to kill you, so leave [the city]; indeed, I am to you of the sincere advisors
commentary:
وَجَآءَ رَجُلٌ مِّنْ أَقْصَا ٱلْمَدِينَةِ يَسْعَىٰ قَالَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّ ٱلْمَلَأَ يَأْتَمِرُونَ بِكَ لِيَقْتُلُوكَ فَٱخْرُجْ إِنِّى لَكَ مِنَ ٱلنَّـٰصِحِينَ

وَجَآءَ رَجُلٌ مِّنْ أَقْصَا ٱلْمَدِينَةِ - a man came from the farthest end of the city. The palace was at the far end, requiring a long journey through traffic to reach where Musa (AS) was. يَسْعَىٰ - he rushed, he hurried.

قَالَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّ ٱلْمَلَأَ يَأْتَمِرُونَ بِكَ - "Musa, the chiefs are deliberating about you." The word يَأْتَمِرُونَ means to pass a verdict, to determine punitive measures. The generals held a meeting in the castle to decide your punishment.

لِيَقْتُلُوكَ - to kill you. The death warrant has been processed. You are to be killed on sight. فَٱخْرُجْ - "Get out of town now!"

Who was telling him this? An Egyptian friend. But Musa (AS) had just encountered an Egyptian trying to kill an Israelite, and that Israelite turned out to be a criminal too. His faith in people was shaken. Why would someone from the palace want to help an Israelite? Why should I trust anyone anymore?

So even hearing this urgent warning, Musa (AS) hesitated. The man had to convince him: إِنِّى لَكَ مِنَ ٱلنَّـٰصِحِينَ - "I am certainly among those who mean well for you. Don't assume that because I'm from the other ethnicity I don't care about you. Have you forgotten who I am? Get your act together!"

This reveals the evil of nationalism and ethnic antagonism. When nations are set against each other, you stop seeing the other person as a human being first - you see them only as a member of that regime, that nationality, and therefore untrustworthy. But this man broke through that barrier to warn Musa (AS).

translation: So he left it, fearful and anticipating [apprehension]. He said, "My Lord, save me from the wrongdoing people
commentary:
فَخَرَجَ مِنْهَا خَآئِفًا يَتَرَقَّبُ ۖ قَالَ رَبِّ نَجِّنِى مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

فَخَرَجَ مِنْهَا خَآئِفًا يَتَرَقَّبُ - so he left from there, fearful and watching carefully. Once again, the same state of vigilant alertness, but this time leaving the entire city. He learned his lesson - last time he almost got drawn in again to help someone. Not this time. He would watch himself, blend in, move carefully, remain undetected.

قَالَ رَبِّ نَجِّنِى مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ - he said, "My Master, save me from the wrongdoing people." This is strikingly similar to the supplication his mother made at the beginning of the story: أَنجِهِ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ - save him from the wrongdoing people. It seems Musa (AS) received some education in how to make supplication, perhaps from his mother during those formative years.

translation: And when he directed himself toward Madyan, he said, "Perhaps my Lord will guide me to the sound way
commentary:
وَلَمَّا تَوَجَّهَ تِلْقَآءَ مَدْيَنَ قَالَ عَسَىٰ رَبِّىٓ أَن يَهْدِيَنِى سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ

وَلَمَّا تَوَجَّهَ تِلْقَآءَ مَدْيَنَ - when he headed in the direction of Madyan. He left the city without knowing where to go, simply facing toward Madyan. Something was placed in his heart - an inspiration drawing him in that direction.

قَالَ عَسَىٰ رَبِّىٓ أَن يَهْدِيَنِى سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ - he said, "Perhaps my Master will guide me to the most level, the most direct path." سَوَاء means the straightest route, the direct line.

He was making supplication because he didn't know directions. "Ya Allah, I need Your GPS. I don't even know where I'm going. Hopefully I'm just headed in the right direction." When you leave the Egyptian cities, you enter vast desert. After traveling through empty desert, what becomes desperately needed? Water.

translation: And when he came to the well of Madyan, he found there a crowd of people watering [their flocks], and he found aside from them two women driving back [their flocks]. He said, "What is your circumstance?" They said, "We do not water until the shepherds dispatch [their flocks]; and our father is an old man
commentary:
وَلَمَّا وَرَدَ مَآءَ مَدْيَنَ وَجَدَ عَلَيْهِ أُمَّةً مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ يَسْقُونَ وَوَجَدَ مِن دُونِهِمُ ٱمْرَأَتَيْنِ تَذُودَانِ ۖ قَالَ مَا خَطْبُكُمَا ۖ قَالَتَا لَا نَسْقِى حَتَّىٰ يُصْدِرَ ٱلرِّعَآءُ ۖ وَأَبُونَا شَيْخٌ كَبِيرٌ

وَلَمَّا وَرَدَ مَآءَ مَدْيَنَ - when he arrived at the water of Madyan. Finally, he found water. As he was drinking, he noticed a large crowd of shepherds giving their flocks water. أُمَّة مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ يَسْقُونَ - a group of people watering their animals. All their sheep, goats and cows were drinking at the water's edge.

وَوَجَدَ مِن دُونِهِمُ ٱمْرَأَتَيْنِ تَذُودَانِ - and he found, separate from them, two women holding back their animals. تَذُودَانِ means to hold back with difficulty, almost wrestling. The sheep could see the water and desperately wanted to drink, pulling toward it, while these girls were yanking them back. It was like a tug-of-war between the sheep and the young women, and the sheep were winning.

Musa (AS) observed this strange scene from a distance. He drank some water, regained his strength, and his character wouldn't allow him to simply watch. He went over and asked directly: قَالَ مَا خَطْبُكُمَا - "What is your situation? What's your problem?"

This is the same directness we saw in Ibrahim (AS). No flowery introduction, no "O ladies of great strength, might I assist you?" Just straightforward: what's wrong? He noticed all these men doing what was clearly men's work, yet these two women were struggling up on the hill. Why?

قَالَتَا - they both spoke together. One didn't speak while the other remained silent - they found strength in each other and responded in unison. Notice what's absent: no giggling, no small talk, no "Oh sir, we're just trying to..." None of that. They were completely professional and straightforward.

لَا نَسْقِى حَتَّىٰ يُصْدِرَ ٱلرِّعَآءُ - "We cannot water our flock until the shepherds finish and take their flocks away." They explained they didn't want to mix with those men, pushing and shoving among them. They would wait until everyone was done. The subtext: we don't want to be in close physical proximity with non-mahram men.

وَأَبُونَا شَيْخٌ كَبِيرٌ - "And our father is an elderly man of great authority." شَيْخ can mean old, but also implies experience, knowledge and status. They weren't just explaining why they were working - they were also establishing: "If you have any bad intentions, you should know our father has significant authority. Watch yourself."

They spoke in a defensive tone, protecting themselves while explaining their situation.

translation: So he watered [their flocks] for them; then he went back to the shade and said, "My Lord, indeed I am, for whatever good You would send down to me, in need
commentary:
فَسَقَىٰ لَهُمَا ثُمَّ تَوَلَّىٰٓ إِلَى ٱلظِّلِّ فَقَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى لِمَآ أَنزَلْتَ إِلَىَّ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَقِيرٌ

Musa (AS) didn't make any small talk in response. No "How far is your home? This must be difficult. How about this weather?" Nothing. He wasn't there for conversation. فَسَقَىٰ لَهُمَا - he simply grabbed all their sheep and walked away.

The girls must have watched in amazement as this exceptionally strong man pushed through the crowd of men like a tree moving through the forest. He watered all their animals, brought them back, handed over the sheep without a word - no "you're welcome," no waiting for thanks, no price for his assistance - and walked away.

ثُمَّ تَوَلَّىٰٓ إِلَى ٱلظِّلِّ - then he returned to the shade. After his long journey, he had finally found a tree and was relaxing. Then he saw those shepherds and noticed the two women struggling, and something made him get up despite his exhaustion. Now he returned to that tree and sat back down.

What motivated him to interrupt his rest? He explains in his supplication:

فَقَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى لِمَآ أَنزَلْتَ إِلَىَّ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَقِيرٌ - he said, "My Master, indeed I am in need of whatever good You send down to me." فَقِيرٌ literally means one whose backbone is broken by burden, difficulty or poverty - completely bankrupt.

"Ya Allah, I am bankrupt of any good. Any opportunity for good deeds, I am desperately in need of it." This wasn't a supplication for a house, a job or a wife. His first thought was: I need to be doing good deeds, because good deeds bring about a good life. "I saw that opportunity and I got up and did it, even though that shade was the best rest I've had in a long time. But I can't relax until I've done something good. Ya Allah, if You have other opportunities, send them my way, because I need them. I am desperately in need of any good deed I can perform."

What is the lesson for us? Don't overlook any opportunity to do a good deed. If someone as spiritually elevated as Musa (AS) considered himself bankrupt of good, what about us? We cannot afford to squander any chance to perform acts of kindness and service.

translation: Then one of the two women came to him walking with shyness. She said, "Indeed, my father invites you that he may reward you for having watered for us." So when he came to him and related to him the story, he said, "Fear not. You have escaped from the wrongdoing people
commentary:
فَجَآءَتْهُ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا تَمْشِى عَلَى ٱسْتِحْيَآءٍ قَالَتْ إِنَّ أَبِى يَدْعُوكَ لِيَجْزِيَكَ أَجْرَ مَا سَقَيْتَ لَنَا ۚ فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُۥ وَقَصَّ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْقَصَصَ قَالَ لَا تَخَفْ ۖ نَجَوْتَ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

The girls returned home hours earlier than usual - they typically had to wait until everyone else finished before watering their flock. When they arrived early with all the animals watered, their father immediately knew something was different. They explained what happened, and a conversation unfolded that we must imagine.

The father asked careful questions: "A strange man approached you?" He was protective, concerned. "What exactly did he say? Tell me his precise words."

"He just asked what our problem was. He didn't make any comments. He didn't ask about us."

"Where were his eyes?"

"Looking down the whole time. He didn't look at us directly."

"Are you certain?"

"Yes, we're sure. We work outside, we know what men are like. Why do you think we stay back when the men are there?"

"Then what did he do?"

"He took our sheep, watered them, brought them back."

"Did he ask for money?"

"No."

"Did he ask your names? Where you live? How old you are? Whether you're married?"

"No. Nothing."

"What did he do then?"

"He looked like he was praying. He went back and sat under a tree."

The father made his assessment. This was a man of good character. قَالَ (qala) - he told one of his daughters to invite the man to their home.

فَجَآءَتْهُ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا - one of the two came to him. Which one? The Quran deliberately doesn't tell us. This protects their dignity and chastity. We don't get to speculate about which sister it was, whether she was the older or younger, or which one might have been interested in him.

Now the girl had thoughts running through her head: Why is my father calling him? Why did he send me? تَمْشِى عَلَى ٱسْتِحْيَآءٍ- she walked with modesty, with shyness. اِسْتِحْيَاء implies several things: modesty in character, natural shyness, and حَيَاء - guarding one's shame, protecting one's heart.

She was extra cautious. Before, she had been with her sister, but now she was alone. She didn't walk directly toward him or in a way that seemed too friendly. She kept a distance. She may have approached from the side so he wouldn't see her coming directly. She walked seriously and deliberately.

قَالَتْ إِنَّ أَبِى يَدْعُوكَ - she said, "Indeed, my father calls you." She emphasized: إِنَّ أَبِى - it's definitely my father, don't get any ideas. It's my father for sure who's calling you.

لِيَجْزِيَكَ أَجْرَ مَا سَقَيْتَ لَنَا - "To compensate you fully for what you watered for us." The phrase لِيَجْزِيَكَ أَجْرَ is interesting - جَزَى means compensation, and أَجْر also means compensation or full payment. She was nervous, somewhat tripping over her words: "To pay you... the compensation... the full compensation for the work... for the watering... that you did for us."

Notice what Musa (AS) didn't do. He didn't respond with false modesty: "No, no, it's okay. I don't need anything. It's beneath me." He had just made supplication: رَبِّ إِنِّى لِمَآ أَنزَلْتَ إِلَىَّ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَقِيرٌ - "Whatever good You send my way, I'll take it." So when an opportunity came, he simply went. Your father wants to compensate me? I didn't do it for payment, but okay, let's go.

We don't even find description of how they walked or where they went. The narrative jumps directly to: فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُۥ وَقَصَّ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْقَصَصَ - when he came to him and told him the stories. Musa (AS) related everything: when I was a child, this happened. This is who Fir'awn is. This is what goes on in Egypt. When I grew up, I used to go into the city to help people. There was this troublemaker - he makes me so angry. Then the next morning that same guy showed up again. I was just trying to help him, and look what happened.

He told the complete story. And who was listening from the other room? The two daughters were listening to everything. These stories made it clear that Musa (AS) was a fugitive who had committed manslaughter, made mistakes, was running from the law.

If the father had been considering him as a prospect for his daughters - and this town clearly had men of low character who wouldn't help struggling women, who would make inappropriate comments - when he heard this story, that prospect might seem to disappear. An accidental killer running from the authorities?

But the father was a man of wisdom. قَالَ لَا تَخَفْ ۖ نَجَوْتَ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ - he said, "Don't be afraid. You have escaped from the wrongdoing people."

What this also means: we are not wrongdoers. You're in good hands here. The father could read people, had wisdom and discernment. He heard the story from his daughters, made a good judgment to invite him, then heard Musa's complete account and made his assessment: this is a man of good character who made a mistake in a corrupt system.

translation: One of the women said, "O my father, hire him. Indeed, the best one you can hire is the strong and the trustworthy
commentary:
قَالَتْ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا يَـٰٓأَبَتِ ٱسْتَـْٔجِرْهُ ۖ إِنَّ خَيْرَ مَنِ ٱسْتَـْٔجَرْتَ ٱلْقَوِىُّ ٱلْأَمِينُ

قَالَتْ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا - one of them said. Again, which one spoke? Was it the one who went to get him, or the other sister? Allah ﷻ doesn't tell us, protecting them from speculation and gossip. If we knew which one, people might say, "Oh, she walked with him and then she liked him, so she talked to her father..." We don't get to make those assumptions.

يَـٰٓأَبَتِ - "O my beloved father." ٱسْتَـْٔجِرْهُ - "Hire him!"

We're learning something profound here: daughters have a voice in the household. They can give business advice. They're not told to go play with dolls while the men make decisions. These daughters were running part of the business - doing the shepherding, bringing in this potential employee. They had direct input into business decisions, and their father took their counsel seriously because they were a critical part of the household operation.

إِنَّ خَيْرَ مَنِ ٱسْتَـْٔجَرْتَ ٱلْقَوِىُّ ٱلْأَمِينُ - "Indeed, the best of those you can hire is the strong, the trustworthy."

Usually such a statement would be general advice, but they present it as though this specific combination is extraordinarily rare. "The best kind of person to hire is one who is strong and honest - and this man is the only one of his kind. Where are you going to find another person like this?"

The subtext continues: "Frankly, we're tired of wrestling sheep. You've been looking for someone to hire, but you don't trust men around us. This man opened his entire story to you, however embarrassing. He's trustworthy. He even instructed me to walk behind him, not beside him. If you can trust him around us, you need to hire him. It'll be a huge help to us, and if people see him around, we won't be as vulnerable."

Here we learn the two essential qualities for hiring anyone: Are they strong enough - do they have the right qualifications - to do the job? And can you trust them? Which is harder to find? Trust. You can find resumes and qualifications everywhere. A trustworthy employee is rare. If you find someone with integrity, even if they're initially weak in skills, hire them and train them, because trustworthiness is the difficult quality to find.

translation: He said, "Indeed, I wish to wed you one of these, my two daughters, on [the condition] that you serve me for eight years; but if you complete ten, it will be [as a favor] from you. And I do not wish to put you in difficulty. You will find me, if Allah wills, from among the righteous
commentary:
قَالَ إِنِّىٓ أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُنكِحَكَ إِحْدَى ٱبْنَتَىَّ هَـٰتَيْنِ عَلَىٰٓ أَن تَأْجُرَنِى ثَمَـٰنِىَ حِجَجٍ ۖ فَإِنْ أَتْمَمْتَ عَشْرًا فَمِنْ عِندِكَ ۖ وَمَآ أُرِيدُ أَنْ أَشُقَّ عَلَيْكَ ۚ سَتَجِدُنِىٓ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

قَالَ إِنِّىٓ أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُنكِحَكَ إِحْدَى ٱبْنَتَىَّ هَـٰتَيْنِ - he said, "Indeed I intend to marry you to one of these two daughters of mine."

The girls were thinking their father would take their advice and hire this man. Instead, the father says he intends to get him married! إِنِّىٓ أُرِيدُ - "I have no doubt in my mind, I'm clear about this: one of my daughters is going to marry you. That's my proposal, my friend. I want you as my son-in-law."

Now both daughters are thinking: which one? But the father maintains the mystery. He doesn't point and say, "That one, right? The one who called me over?" He keeps it general: إِحْدَى ٱبْنَتَىَّ - one of my two daughters. This is brilliant - the young man doesn't get ideas, doesn't start making assumptions or special preparations.

Notice also: this is the father proposing on behalf of his daughters to the young man. In many Muslim cultures, families will wait indefinitely for a proposal to arrive, considering it shameful to seek out a good prospect. But here we see a father who observed a man of good character and made a direct proposal.

This is a valid approach, especially in times when good, practicing young men are hard to find. If a father prays at the masjid, sees a young man praying regularly, learns he's studying or working responsibly, there's no shame in approaching him: "I've been thinking - let me talk to your parents. I was considering you for my daughter." Especially when good character is rare, when you find it, secure it.

There's another wisdom here. The father recognized: if I hire you, you'll be working here long-term. You'll be in proximity to my daughters regularly. That's not healthy - Shaytan plays his games in such situations. He's a smart man, so he addresses this directly with a proposal of marriage.

The father also understood this young man had nowhere to go, no family, no support system. He was offering a comprehensive solution.

عَلَىٰٓ أَن تَأْجُرَنِى ثَمَـٰنِىَ حِجَجٍ - "On the condition that you work for me for eight years." This doesn't mean "work eight years and then marry her." It means: marry her now, but you'll work for me for eight years. I'm hiring you for eight years, which means you have job security.

The father-in-law isn't extracting labor - he's paying him, employing him. Musa (AS) gets a steady job, a place to live, a family, and an honest employer all at once. This is as much a favor to Musa (AS) as it is beneficial for the family. The father found a solution good for everyone: good for Musa (AS), good for his daughter, good for his household, good all around.

He knows this young man needs stability - young men need structure and security. So if your father-in-law offers you employment and you can't find work elsewhere, it's not beneath you. Take it. This is an honorable arrangement between two men.

Notice something beautiful: when describing the years, he said حِجَجٍ, which comes from حَجّ. The people of Madyan measured years by the season of Hajj. They knew about the Ka'bah, knew it was built by Ibrahim (AS) for the purpose of pilgrimage. 

This means Musa (AS), considered the father of the Jewish faith, spent eight to ten years around people who knew about the Ka'bah, who knew about Ibrahim (AS) and his legacy. When the Quran tells the Jews "you know this like you know your own children," this knowledge goes back to the very beginning. The word "hajj" was used in Musa's time, connecting to Ibrahim (AS) and the sacred house in Makkah.

فَإِنْ أَتْمَمْتَ عَشْرًا فَمِنْ عِندِكَ - "And if you complete ten years, that would be from yourself" - meaning that would be generous of you, a kindness.

Wait - eight years or ten? The father is an excellent negotiator. From Musa's perspective, eight years is already a major commitment. So the father adds: وَمَآ أُرِيدُ أَنْ أَشُقَّ عَلَيْكَ - "And I don't intend to put hardship upon you. I'm not going to overwork you. I know you're strong, but I won't take advantage of that. I'm not burdening you with this. I know it's a lot - marriage, employment, eight years. This is your decision. If you don't want it, that's fine. I'm not forcing anything on you."

This is masterful negotiation. He makes it seem completely like Musa's choice, lays it out so well that when Musa (AS) thinks about it himself, he realizes there's really no other good option - but because it feels like his own decision rather than something forced upon him, he can accept it genuinely.

There's a lesson here for marriage negotiations today. Don't seem desperate, either side. If the girl's family is too eager, calling every day asking "Have you decided? What do you think? Have you reached a decision yet?" - the other side starts thinking, "This is already guaranteed. Let me see what other options are out there." Smart negotiation is: present a good proposal, give them time to think, then let them come to their own decision. If they haven't called back in a week, they start thinking, "It's a good family. We haven't responded. We don't want to lose this opportunity."

The father concludes: سَتَجِدُنِىٓ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ - "You will find me, if Allah wills, among the righteous." In other words: I'll be a good father-in-law, a good employer. I'll pay you fairly. I won't pay you scraps just because you're living in the household. I won't take advantage of you being a live-in son-in-law. You'll find me to be a person of good character.

This entire conversation is remarkable. The daughters are likely in another room, listening in shock and amazement at how this unfolded. And Musa (AS) is about to give his response - but that is a story for another time.

translation: [Moses] said, "That is [established] between me and you. Whichever of the two terms I complete - there is no injustice to me, and Allah, over what we say, is Witness
commentary:
قَالَ ذَٰلِكَ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكَ ۖ أَيَّمَا ٱلْأَجَلَيْنِ قَضَيْتُ فَلَا عُدْوَٰنَ عَلَىَّ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ مَا نَقُولُ وَكِيلٌ

The father has proposed his marriage arrangement: one of his daughters will marry Musa (AS), provided he works for him eight to ten years. Remarkably, the father doesn't come across as desperate. He doesn't simply say, "You're a good man, marry my daughter." Instead, he establishes clear terms that honor his daughter's value. This teaches us something important about the mahr in marriage. It should be respectable and appropriate to your circumstances. While we shouldn't make it excessive or burdensome, we also shouldn't trivialize it.

Some people quote the Sahaba (RA), who accepted dates or memorized portions of Quran as their mahr. But we must remember those companions had given everything for the sake of Allah ﷻ. They were in desperate economic situations. If you're living a normal life with means, don't use their economic situation to justify minimizing the mahr while ignoring their sacrifice in every other aspect of faith. The amount must be decent and respectable.

Notice also that this wasn't purely a monetary arrangement. It was service-based. You might think the father was the only beneficiary, but he was actually helping Musa (AS) by hiring him. He was taking care of him and ensuring his daughter would have stable livelihood. It was a mutually beneficial arrangement that this wise father designed.

When the father says ذَٰلِكَ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكَ (that is between me and you), this becomes the only marriage negotiation mentioned in the Quran. And significantly, Allah ﷻ chose to highlight a negotiation between the son-in-law and the father. Both parties must be involved. Marriage is not something to be arranged entirely by parents while the groom sits in another room wondering what's happening. The husband needs to be part of these discussions because he's the one who will fulfill these obligations.

أَيَّمَا ٱلْأَجَلَيْنِ قَضَيْتُ فَلَا عُدْوَٰنَ عَلَىَّ (Whichever of the two terms I fulfill, there is no blame upon me) – The father leaves the choice open. Musa (AS) doesn't commit to ten years specifically. This is wisdom on his part. Perhaps eight years will prove difficult and he'll want to leave, or perhaps they'll be good years and extending to ten will be natural. By leaving it open, neither party can complain later. The father can't pressure him to stay beyond eight, and if Musa (AS) chooses to stay longer, it's his choice.

وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ مَا نَقُولُ وَكِيلٌ (And Allah is Witness over what we say) – They make Allah ﷻ the Wakil, the guardian and executor of this contract. This acknowledges a profound truth: whether they live to see these eight or ten years is entirely in Allah's hands. Whether this marriage happens, whether either of them survives, whether Musa (AS) remains able to work – all of this is with Allah ﷻ. So while they make their agreement, they submit the outcome to the One who truly controls all affairs.

translation: And when Moses had completed the term and was traveling with his family, he perceived from the direction of the mount a fire. He said to his family, "Stay here; indeed, I have perceived a fire. Perhaps I will bring you from there [some] information or burning wood from the fire that you may warm yourselves
commentary:
۞ فَلَمَّا قَضَىٰ مُوسَى ٱلْأَجَلَ وَسَارَ بِأَهْلِهِۦٓ ءَانَسَ مِن جَانِبِ ٱلطُّورِ نَارًا قَالَ لِأَهْلِهِ ٱمْكُثُوٓا۟ إِنِّىٓ ءَانَسْتُ نَارًا لَّعَلِّىٓ ءَاتِيكُم مِّنْهَا بِخَبَرٍ أَوْ جَذْوَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَصْطَلُونَ

When Musa (AS) fulfilled the term – notice Allah ﷻ doesn't specify whether he completed eight or ten years. This is the mercy of Allah's wisdom. If He had said ten years, fathers-in-law everywhere would say the better thing is to demand more. If He had said eight, sons-in-law would argue that less is better. By leaving it unspecified, Allah ﷻ keeps this negotiation balanced and leaves room for people to work out what's appropriate for their circumstances.

وَسَارَ بِأَهْلِهِۦٓ (and traveled with his family) – The word سَارَ is significant. It indicates casual, easy travel. There was no drama when Musa (AS) moved away with his wife. The father-in-law didn't create a scene. The mother-in-law didn't fall into depression or cry uncontrollably. They let him go easily because this is natural. When a man marries and needs to relocate for work or other reasons, his wife is now part of his household. He has to make the best decisions for his family unit. Extended families shouldn't burden young couples with guilt over necessary moves.

Notice Allah ﷻ calls them أَهْلِهِۦٓ (his family) – she is now his family, marking her transition from her father's household to her husband's protection and care.

ءَانَسَ مِن جَانِبِ ٱلطُّورِ نَارًا (he saw from the side of the Mount a fire) – At this moment, we're being given details we've never received before in the Quran. Until now, every time Musa's story was mentioned, it began with his prophethood or his mission to Fir'awn. This surah gives us the prequel – what happened before, the formative experiences that shaped Musa (AS). It's like when stories take you back to show you the early years, the origin story. Allah ﷻ is showing us what led to that moment at the mountain.

When Musa (AS) tells his family إِنِّىٓ ءَانَسْتُ نَارًا (Indeed, I have perceived a fire), this is actually the earlier, more uncertain conversation. In Surah Taha, the wording was more confident. Here, he's less optimistic: لَّعَلِّىٓ ءَاتِيكُم مِّنْهَا بِخَبَرٍ أَوْ جَذْوَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ (Perhaps I will bring you from there some information or a burning coal). A جَذْوَة is an ember that's almost gone out – you can see dots of red, but it's mostly turning to ash. He's saying maybe he can revive some spark from whatever little fire remains. The tone is cautious, uncertain about what he'll find.

translation: But when he came to it, he was called from the right side of the valley in a blessed spot - from the tree, "O Moses, indeed I am Allah, Lord of the worlds
commentary:
فَلَمَّآ أَتَىٰهَا نُودِىَ مِن شَـٰطِئِ ٱلْوَادِ ٱلْأَيْمَنِ فِى ٱلْبُقْعَةِ ٱلْمُبَـٰرَكَةِ مِنَ ٱلشَّجَرَةِ أَن يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنِّىٓ أَنَا ٱللَّهُ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

When he came to it, he was called from the right side of the valley, from within the blessed spot, from the tree. Allah ﷻ is giving us geographical precision: the location, the specific side of the valley, the blessed patch of land. But notice something: the previous ayah mentioned fire, yet this ayah makes no mention of fire. Instead, Allah ﷻ emphasizes the location and that the voice came مِنَ ٱلشَّجَرَةِ (from the tree).

We know from other passages that fire had engulfed this tree, but miraculously, instead of destroying it, the fire was giving it life. The tree was sprouting and growing despite being surrounded by flames. The blessed nature of this location – ٱلْبُقْعَةِ ٱلْمُبَـٰرَكَةِ (the blessed spot) – was manifested in this supernatural phenomenon.

يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنِّىٓ أَنَا ٱللَّهُ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (O Musa, indeed I am Allah, Lord of the worlds) – The divine introduction is direct and powerful. Musa (AS) came seeking warmth for his family and instead encountered the Lord of all creation addressing him by name.

translation: And [he was told], "Throw down your staff." But when he saw it writhing as if it was a snake, he turned in flight and did not return. [Allah said], "O Moses, approach and fear not. Indeed, you are of the secure
commentary:
وَأَنْ أَلْقِ عَصَاكَ ۖ فَلَمَّا رَءَاهَا تَهْتَزُّ كَأَنَّهَا جَآنٌّ وَلَّىٰ مُدْبِرًا وَلَمْ يُعَقِّبْ ۚ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ أَقْبِلْ وَلَا تَخَفْ ۖ إِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْـَٔامِنِينَ

The command comes: throw down your staff. When he saw it moving كَأَنَّهَا جَآنٌّ (as if it were a snake), he turned and fled وَلَمْ يُعَقِّبْ (and did not return). This is a natural human reaction to something terrifying and inexplicable.

But then comes the reassuring call: يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ أَقْبِلْ وَلَا تَخَفْ (O Musa, approach and fear not). The word أَقْبِلْ means to face something, to come toward it with your face. Don't turn your back – face this. This is training. Musa (AS) will soon have to face a tyrannical king, and he cannot back down. He must learn to confront what terrifies him.

إِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْـَٔامِنِينَ (Indeed, you are of the secure) – This isn't just reassurance for this moment. It's a promise for what's to come. You are among those who will be kept safe. Throughout your mission, no matter how threatening the situations become, you will remain protected. This promise of security becomes the foundation of his courage.

translation: Insert your hand into the opening of your garment; it will come out white, without disease. And draw in your arm close to you [as prevention] from fear, for those are two proofs from your Lord to Pharaoh and his establishment. Indeed, they have been a people defiantly disobedient
commentary:
ٱسْلُكْ يَدَكَ فِى جَيْبِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضَآءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوٓءٍ وَٱضْمُمْ إِلَيْكَ جَنَاحَكَ مِنَ ٱلرَّهْبِ ۖ فَذَٰنِكَ بُرْهَـٰنَانِ مِن رَّبِّكَ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ

Insert your hand into your collar and it will come out white without disease. The miracle is immediate and undeniable – not a skin condition, but a supernatural radiance. Then comes a beautiful instruction: وَٱضْمُمْ إِلَيْكَ جَنَاحَكَ مِنَ ٱلرَّهْبِ (and draw your arm to yourself from fear).

This is remarkable. Allah ﷻ is teaching Musa (AS) how to manage his fear. When you become frightened, put your hand back. The glowing hand goes back into your garment, close to your heart, and the fear subsides. It's almost like an off-switch for the miracle, but also a technique for self-calming. If you get overwhelmed, you can turn it off.

فَذَٰنِكَ بُرْهَـٰنَانِ (These are two proofs) – The word بُرْهَـٰن signifies overwhelming evidence, undeniable proof. Not mere signs, but demonstrations of divine power that leave no room for doubt. These two miracles – the staff and the hand – are meant to establish Musa's authority before Fir'awn and his inner circle.

إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ (Indeed, they have been a people defiantly disobedient) – The word فَـٰسِق indicates someone who has broken out of the bounds of decency and righteousness. Fir'awn and his elite have crossed every limit, which is why such powerful signs are necessary.

translation: He said, "My Lord, indeed, I killed from among them someone, and I fear they will kill me
commentary:
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى قَتَلْتُ مِنْهُمْ نَفْسًا فَأَخَافُ أَن يَقْتُلُونِ

Musa (AS) responds with his concern: My Master, I killed someone among them, so I fear they will kill me. This is honest vulnerability before Allah ﷻ. He's not refusing the mission, but he's expressing a legitimate fear based on his past. There's a warrant out for his arrest. He's a wanted man who fled Egypt as a fugitive. Now he's being told to return to face the very people who want him dead.

This teaches us that expressing our fears to Allah ﷻ is not a lack of faith. Musa (AS), one of the greatest prophets, openly admits his terror. But he's doing so in dua, in conversation with his Lord, seeking help and reassurance.

translation: And my brother Aaron is more fluent than me in tongue, so send him with me as support, verifying me. Indeed, I fear that they will deny me
commentary:
وَأَخِى هَـٰرُونُ هُوَ أَفْصَحُ مِنِّى لِسَانًا فَأَرْسِلْهُ مَعِىَ رِدْءًا يُصَدِّقُنِىٓ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ أَن يُكَذِّبُونِ

My brother Harun is more eloquent in speech than I am. The word أَفْصَحُ captures smoothness and clarity of expression. Send him with me as رِدْءًا – a supporting structure, like a wall that keeps something from falling, or the support beams behind a billboard that hold it upright.

Musa (AS) has already referred to his brother using different terms in other passages – مُؤَازِرًا (helper) and now رِدْءًا (support). He's emphasizing how desperately he needs backup. يُصَدِّقُنِىٓ (to confirm me) – at the very least, I need someone who believes in me. It's powerful that even prophets need someone who has their back, someone who validates them and stands with them.

إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ أَن يُكَذِّبُونِ (Indeed I fear they will deny me) – Again, the honest admission of fear. Musa (AS) knows that his speech impediment combined with his criminal record will make people dismiss him as a liar. Having his brother there would provide credibility and emotional support.

translation: [Allah] said, "We will strengthen your arm through your brother and grant you both supremacy so they will not reach you. [It will be] through Our signs; you and those who follow you will be the predominant
commentary:
قَالَ سَنَشُدُّ عَضُدَكَ بِأَخِيكَ وَنَجْعَلُ لَكُمَا سُلْطَـٰنًا فَلَا يَصِلُونَ إِلَيْكُمَا ۚ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أَنتُمَا وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَكُمَا ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ

Allah ﷻ responds to both concerns. سَنَشُدُّ عَضُدَكَ بِأَخِيكَ (We will strengthen your arm through your brother) – the imagery is of warriors preparing for battle, wrapping and reinforcing the arm. Your brother will be like additional muscle, additional strength.

وَنَجْعَلُ لَكُمَا سُلْطَـٰنًا (And We will make for you an authority) – not worldly political power, but a supernatural authority that comes from divine backing. فَلَا يَصِلُونَ إِلَيْكُمَا (so they will not reach you) – they won't be able to harm you, won't be able to kill you.

Then comes an even more encouraging promise: أَنتُمَا وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَكُمَا ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ (You and those who follow you will be the predominant). Musa (AS) was worried about being called a liar and having no one believe him. Allah ﷻ is telling him: Don't worry about followers. You will have followers. And it is only because of Our signs that you, your brother, and your followers will dominate and prevail.

translation: But when Moses came to them with Our signs as clear evidences, they said, "This is not except invented magic, and we have not heard of this [religion] among our forefathers
commentary:
فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم مُّوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ قَالُوا۟ مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّفْتَرًى وَمَا سَمِعْنَا بِهَـٰذَا فِىٓ ءَابَآئِنَا ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

When Musa (AS) came to them with Our signs that were بَيِّنَـٰت (clear and self-evident), their response was predictable: this is nothing but fabricated magic. The word مُّفْتَرًى indicates something creative and made up, suggesting Musa (AS) invented these tricks himself.

Their second objection reveals their attachment to tradition: وَمَا سَمِعْنَا بِهَـٰذَا فِىٓ ءَابَآئِنَا ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (We have not heard of this among our forefathers). This is the argument of those who reject truth simply because it differs from what they inherited. They worship the past rather than seeking truth. If their ancestors didn't believe it or practice it, then it must be false. This kind of reasoning makes ancestors into infallible authorities and closes the door to truth.

translation: And Moses said, "My Lord is more knowing [than we or you] of who has come with guidance from Him and to whom will be succession in the home. Indeed, wrongdoers do not succeed
commentary:
وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰ رَبِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن جَآءَ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ مِنْ عِندِهِۦ وَمَن تَكُونُ لَهُۥ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلدَّارِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ
