Musa's response is measured and dignified: My Lord is more knowing of who has come with guidance from Him. He doesn't argue or try to prove himself through debate. Instead, he redirects to Allah ﷻ as the ultimate judge of truth.

وَمَن تَكُونُ لَهُۥ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلدَّارِ (and who will have the [best] outcome of the home) – this isn't just about punishment. The word دَار (home) represents the final dwelling, the ultimate destination. Who will have the good home in the end? That's what matters.

إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (Indeed, wrongdoers do not succeed) – This is a principle, a law of existence. The path of injustice and oppression never leads to real success. It might create temporary power or wealth, but ultimate success belongs only to those who live by truth and justice.

translation: And Pharaoh said, "O eminent ones, I have not known you to have a god other than me. Then ignite for me, O Haman, [a fire] upon the clay and make for me a tower that I may look at the God of Moses. And indeed, I do think he is among the liars
commentary:
وَقَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَأُ مَا عَلِمْتُ لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرِى فَأَوْقِدْ لِى يَـٰهَـٰمَـٰنُ عَلَى ٱلطِّينِ فَٱجْعَل لِّى صَرْحًا لَّعَلِّىٓ أَطَّلِعُ إِلَىٰٓ إِلَـٰهِ مُوسَىٰ وَإِنِّى لَأَظُنُّهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ

Fir'awn's response is breathtaking in its arrogance. يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَأُ مَا عَلِمْتُ لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرِى (O eminent ones, I have not known you to have a god other than me). The phrasing is interesting – he frames it as though he's heard rumors that they might be worshiping someone else, and he's correcting this misunderstanding. "Let me set the record straight: there is no god for you besides me."

Then he turns to his chief minister: يَـٰهَـٰمَـٰنُ (O Haman), light a fire and bake clay bricks. Make me a tower. His logic is mockery: Musa went up a mountain, saw a fire, and claimed to speak to God. So Fir'awn will build his own fire and his own tower. He'll climb up himself and check if this God of Musa exists.

لَّعَلِّىٓ أَطَّلِعُ إِلَىٰٓ إِلَـٰهِ مُوسَىٰ (that I may look at the God of Musa) – This is theatrical. He doesn't believe for a second, but he's making a public spectacle to humiliate the message. When he builds his tower and climbs it and finds nothing, he'll tell the people: "See? I checked for you. There's no god up there. Musa is lying."

وَإِنِّى لَأَظُنُّهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (And indeed, I think he is of the liars) – If you think he's a liar, why build the tower? Because it's not about seeking truth. It's about making a public display of dismissing truth. Islam is spreading, people are being affected, so Fir'awn needs a dramatic way to discredit the message.

translation: And he was arrogant, he and his soldiers, in the land, without right, and they thought that they would not be returned to Us
commentary:
وَٱسْتَكْبَرَ هُوَ وَجُنُودُهُۥ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْنَا لَا يُرْجَعُونَ

Notice how Allah ﷻ singles out Fir'awn's arrogance before mentioning his army. وَٱسْتَكْبَرَ هُوَ (And he was arrogant, he himself) – his personal arrogance is highlighted. Then وَجُنُودُهُۥ (and his soldiers). He could have just said "they were arrogant," but by separating Fir'awn first, Allah ﷻ shows that his arrogance was of a special, more severe kind that then permeated into his forces.

بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ (without right) – their arrogance had no legitimate basis. They weren't powerful because of their own merit. Whatever power they had was given to them, and they abused it.

وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْنَا لَا يُرْجَعُونَ (And they thought they would not be returned to Us) – This is the root cause. When people become convinced there's no accountability, no final judgment, no return to Allah ﷻ, that's when they speak and act with such obnoxious disregard for truth and decency.

translation: So We took him and his soldiers and threw them into the sea. So see how was the end of the wrongdoers
commentary:
فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُ وَجُنُودَهُۥ فَنَبَذْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

Just as Allah ﷻ singled him out in his grabbing, He does so in his punishment. فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُ وَجُنُودَهُۥ (So We seized him and his soldiers) – We grabbed him specifically, and his army.

فَنَبَذْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ (and threw them into the sea) – The word نَبَذَ means to chuck something worthless, like kicking a bottle cap across the street. We threw them like they were nothing into the river. This is the second time the river plays a central role in this story. The first time, a baby who was expected to drown survived in that river. That baby's survival in the river sealed Fir'awn's fate. How poetic that where the story begins is where it should end. Fir'awn used to kill babies and throw their bodies in the river. It's fitting that he and his army should die the same way, in that same river.

فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (So see how was the end of the wrongdoers) – Allah ﷻ invites us to observe, to reflect on the pattern of history.

translation: And We made them leaders inviting to the Fire, and on the Day of Resurrection they will not be helped
commentary:
وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَدْعُونَ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَا يُنصَرُونَ

We made them leaders – but leaders of what? يَدْعُونَ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ (calling [people] to the Fire). In this life, they were leaders calling people to the fire that Fir'awn built, mocking revelation. In the next life, they are leaders whose followers will join them in Hellfire. This is the painful irony of corrupt leadership: you take people down with you.

وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَا يُنصَرُونَ (And on the Day of Resurrection they will not be helped) – all the armies, all the power, all the supporters will be useless. No one will be able to aid them or intercede for them.

translation: And We caused to overtake them in this world a curse, and on the Day of Resurrection they will be of the despised
commentary:
وَأَتْبَعْنَـٰهُمْ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلدُّنْيَا لَعْنَةً ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ هُم مِّنَ ٱلْمَقْبُوحِينَ

The punishment extends across both worlds. In this worldly life, We made a curse follow them. Their legacy is cursed. Their names are mentioned with disgust. Fir'awn's name has become synonymous with tyranny for thousands of years.

وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ هُم مِّنَ ٱلْمَقْبُوحِينَ (And on the Day of Resurrection they will be among the disgraced) – The word مَقْبُوح means made ugly, deformed, disfigured. Whatever beauty or dignity they had will be stripped away, and they will be among those made repulsive in appearance.

translation: And We gave Moses the Scripture, after We had destroyed the former generations, as enlightenment for the people and guidance and mercy that they might be reminded
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَآ أَهْلَكْنَا ٱلْقُرُونَ ٱلْأُولَىٰ بَصَآئِرَ لِلنَّاسِ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ

This surah does something unique. Usually when Musa's story is told, we hear about Fir'awn and then move on. Here, we learn what happened before Musa's career as a prophet, and now we learn what happened after Fir'awn was destroyed.

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ (And We certainly gave Musa the Scripture) – This came مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَآ أَهْلَكْنَا ٱلْقُرُونَ ٱلْأُولَىٰ (after We had destroyed the former generations). So many civilizations had risen and fallen, leaving behind their palaces and gardens.

The purpose of the book: بَصَآئِرَ لِلنَّاسِ (as enlightenment for the people). The word بَصَآئِر comes from seeing, from insight. Yes, they had seen the staff turn into a snake, they had seen hands turn white, they had seen rivers part with walls of water like mountains. But Allah ﷻ says none of that is like My book. Real insight comes from the Book of Allah ﷻ.

وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً (and guidance and mercy) – The book serves multiple purposes: it enlightens the mind, guides the actions, and brings mercy to the heart. لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (that perhaps they would remember) – perhaps they would be reminded, would reflect, would take heed.

## Ayahs 44-46

وَمَا كُنتَ بِجَانِبِ ٱلْغَرْبِىِّ إِذْ قَضَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَى ٱلْأَمْرَ وَمَا كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ

وَلَـٰكِنَّآ أَنشَأْنَا قُرُونًا فَتَطَاوَلَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْعُمُرُ ۚ وَمَا كُنتَ ثَاوِيًا فِىٓ أَهْلِ مَدْيَنَ تَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا وَلَـٰكِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ

وَمَا كُنتَ بِجَانِبِ ٱلطُّورِ إِذْ نَادَيْنَا وَلَـٰكِن رَّحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ لِتُنذِرَ قَوْمًا مَّآ أَتَىٰهُم مِّن نَّذِيرٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ

Allah ﷻ addresses the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ directly: You weren't on the western side when We gave the command to Musa (AS). You weren't among the witnesses. You weren't raised generation after generation in Madyan, going around reciting Our revelations to them. You weren't at the side of the Mount when We called out.

These detailed accounts – which side of the valley, what Musa (AS) said to his family, how the father-in-law negotiated, what happened in Madyan – how could you know all this? Where did you get these intricate details that no history book records?

وَلَـٰكِنَّآ أَنشَأْنَا قُرُونًا فَتَطَاوَلَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْعُمُرُ (But We produced [many] generations, and prolonged was their duration) – This phrase is crucial and will connect to something later. We raised generations and long periods passed over them. When a nation exists for a very long time, something happens: they become desensitized to their blessings.

Think about a child with a toy. When it's new, they're amazed by it. But when that toy sits on the shelf for years, they don't even look at it anymore. Long periods pass and they lose appreciation for it. Then someone else sees that old toy and is fascinated, and the child says, "Really? You can have it." They've lost all value for it.

Allah ﷻ is describing this phenomenon with religion. When people inherit faith from their parents, who inherited it from their parents, who inherited it from theirs, and centuries pass, the religion becomes empty ritual. They've lost the value for it. It's just something their ancestors did. They're desensitized.

But then someone new discovers Islam – a convert, someone who just learned about it – and everything amazes them. That's in the Quran? That's how you fast? That's the wisdom behind it? They've discovered treasure and they value it immensely.

This sets up what comes next: Allah ﷻ will do something to reignite value for religion in people who've become complacent.

وَلَـٰكِن رَّحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ (But [you are sent] as a mercy from your Lord) – The Prophet ﷺ receiving this revelation, being given these tours of past nations and prophets, is an act of mercy. Why mercy? Let's pause on this word رَحْمَة.

The word mercy in English falls terribly short of what رَحْمَة means. The root of رَحْمَة is رَحِم – the womb of a mother. Allah ﷻ named Himself الرَّحْمَـٰن and الرَّحِيم, connecting His primary attribute to motherhood. In a famous hadith, Allah ﷻ says He created the womb and named it after Himself, and whoever cuts off those family ties, Allah ﷻ cuts them off from His mercy.

Why this connection to mothers? Because the womb takes care of every need of the child. Rent paid, food covered, temperature controlled, perfect rest. Any troubles or pain are borne by the mother. It's the most selfless, pure form of love and care we experience in human life – the love of a mother who gives up her body, almost dies for you, loses sleep for you until the day she dies.

When we say Allah ﷻ is الرَّحِيم, we're saying His care, His love, His concern for our wellbeing, His protection of us is like this and infinitely more. Mercy in English is usually used negatively – the soldiers showed no mercy, meaning they were tyrants but spared this one person. Mercy is about not killing someone who deserved death. That's not what رَحْمَة means.

رَحْمَة encompasses love, compassion, care, concern, protection, nurturing. The Prophet ﷺ said: Can you imagine a mother throwing her child in fire? So why do you think Allah ﷻ wants to throw you in the Fire? Even he compared it to a mother's love, and Allah's love is many times greater.

This revelation to the Prophet ﷺ is an act of that all-encompassing mercy. لِتُنذِرَ قَوْمًا مَّآ أَتَىٰهُم مِّن نَّذِيرٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ (so you may warn a people to whom no warner has come before them) – the Arabs had no warner before. At least let them learn about nations that did have warners, so they can understand what happens when warnings are ignored.

translation: And you, [O Muhammad], were not on the western side [of the mount] when We revealed to Moses the command, and you were not among the witnesses [to that]

But We produced [many] generations [after Moses], and prolonged was their duration. And you were not a resident among the people of Madyan, reciting to them Our verses, but We were senders [of this message]
And you were not at the side of the mount when We called [Moses] but [were sent] as a mercy from your Lord to warn a people to whom no warner had come before you that they might be reminded

And if not that a disaster should strike them for what their hands put forth [of sins] and they would say, "Our Lord, why did You not send us a messenger so we could have followed Your verses and been among the believers
commentary:
وَلَوْلَآ أَن تُصِيبَهُم مُّصِيبَةٌۢ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُوا۟ رَبَّنَا لَوْلَآ أَرْسَلْتَ إِلَيْنَا رَسُولًا فَنَتَّبِعَ ءَايَـٰتِكَ وَنَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

And if not that a disaster should strike them for what their hands put forth and they would say, "Our Lord, why did You not send us a messenger so we could have followed Your verses and been among the believers?" This is about removing excuses. When calamity struck Makkah – when they experienced famine and hardship – they would cry out: Ya Allah, didn't You promise a messenger was coming? Didn't You tell the Jews in their books a final messenger would come? Why didn't You send him so we could follow?

Then when the truth finally came, instead of embracing it, they made excuses. This ayah is cutting off those excuses beforehand. You asked for a messenger. Now you have one. You have no grounds to complain.

translation: But when the truth came to them from Us, they said, "Why was he not given like that which was given to Moses?" Did they not disbelieve in that which was given to Moses before? They said, "[They are but] two works of magic supporting each other, and indeed we are, in both, disbelievers
commentary:
فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِنْ عِندِنَا قَالُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أُوتِىَ مِثْلَ مَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰٓ ۚ أَوَلَمْ يَكْفُرُوا۟ بِمَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ قَالُوا۟ سِحْرَانِ تَظَـٰهَرَا وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا بِكُلٍّ كَـٰفِرُونَ

When the truth came from Us, they said, "Why wasn't he given like what was given to Musa?" Where's his staff? Where's his glowing hand? We want the same miracles Musa (AS) had. We're used to those. This is different and we don't accept it.

Allah ﷻ responds with a powerful question: أَوَلَمْ يَكْفُرُوا۟ بِمَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰ مِن قَبْلُ (Did they not disbelieve in what was given to Musa before?) This cuts several ways. The Fir'awn and his people saw those exact miracles and rejected them. So why are you asking for them now? Did they help them believe?

It could also address the Jews: Aren't you the same people who altered the book and rejected what Musa (AS) was given long ago? And now you're acting like Fir'awn acted, demanding miracles you won't believe anyway.

قَالُوا۟ سِحْرَانِ تَظَـٰهَرَا (They said, "Two works of magic supporting each other") – They're referring to the staff and the hand, calling them magic tricks meant just for show.

وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا بِكُلٍّ كَـٰفِرُونَ (And they said, "Indeed we are disbelievers in all") – We reject everything. We're not buying any of it.

translation: Say, "Then bring a scripture from Allah which is more guiding than either of them that I may follow it, if you should be truthful
commentary:
قُلْ فَأْتُوا۟ بِكِتَـٰبٍ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ هُوَ أَهْدَىٰ مِنْهُمَآ أَتَّبِعْهُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

The Prophet ﷺ is told to issue a challenge: Then bring a scripture from Allah ﷻ that is more guiding than these two – more guiding than the Torah and the Quran together. Show me your book. أَتَّبِعْهُ (I will follow it). This is an open declaration: I'm ready to follow truth wherever it comes from. Bring your evidence.

This is the ultimate confidence in revelation. The Prophet ﷺ isn't afraid of competition because truth doesn't fear examination. If you're truthful, produce it. But you can't, because what you're following isn't revelation at all.

translation: But if they do not respond to you - then know that they only follow their [own] desires. And who is more astray than one who follows his desire without guidance from Allah? Indeed, Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people
commentary:
فَإِن لَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَكَ فَٱعْلَمْ أَنَّمَا يَتَّبِعُونَ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ ۚ وَمَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنِ ٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ بِغَيْرِ هُدًى مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

If they don't respond to you, then know that they are only following their desires. This is the diagnosis. When people can't produce evidence but continue rejecting truth, it's not about evidence. It's about هَوَىٰ – whim, desire, emotion, preference.

وَمَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنِ ٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ بِغَيْرِ هُدًى مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ (And who is more astray than one who follows his desire without guidance from Allah?) There is no one more lost. At least someone who's lost in the desert wants to find the way. But someone following their whims doesn't even want guidance. They've made their desires their god.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (Indeed, Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people) – When you insist on wrong, when you persist in injustice, Allah ﷻ doesn't force guidance on you. Guidance requires some openness, some willingness to accept truth.

translation: And We have [repeatedly] conveyed to them the Qur'an that they might be reminded
commentary:
۞ وَلَقَدْ وَصَّلْنَا لَهُمُ ٱلْقَوْلَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ

And We have certainly conveyed to them the word – وَصَّلْنَا means to join, to connect seamlessly. The word of Allah ﷻ flows like a river into an ocean. Everything connects. The Torah connects to the Quran. The stories connect to each other. The themes flow into one another. This is what وَصَّل means – continuity, connection, one unified message.

For someone knowledgeable in previous scripture, when they encounter Quran, they should recognize this river is coming from the same source. It's the same spring. لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (that perhaps they will be reminded) – those who have any remnant of truth in their hearts will remember, will recognize, will recall the teachings they already knew.

translation: Those to whom We gave the Scripture before it - they are believers in it
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦ هُم بِهِۦ يُؤْمِنُونَ

Those to whom We gave the Scripture before it – they believe in it. This describes sincere followers of previous revelation. When they kept their faith pure, when they studied their books seeking truth not just tradition, they immediately recognized the Quran when it came.

translation: And when it is recited to them, they say, "We have believed in it; indeed, it is the truth from our Lord. Indeed we were, [even] before it, Muslims [submitting to Allah]
commentary:
وَإِذَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِهِۦٓ إِنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا مِن قَبْلِهِۦ مُسْلِمِينَ

When it is recited to them, they say, "We believe in it. Indeed, it is the truth from our Lord." Then comes the beautiful statement: إِنَّا كُنَّا مِن قَبْلِهِۦ مُسْلِمِينَ (Indeed, we were already Muslims before it).

This phrase was used by the Queen of Sheba in the previous surah. When she received Sulayman's letter, that alone was enough. She said the throne would be excessive – I'm already convinced. Similarly, these people of the book say: we had already submitted to Allah ﷻ in our hearts. We didn't know the Quran specifically, but our hearts were already in submission. The impurities in our scripture have now been removed. The truth we were seeking has now been clarified and perfected.

translation: Those will be given their reward twice for what they patiently endured and [because] they avert evil through good, and from what We have provided them they spend
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُؤْتَوْنَ أَجْرَهُم مَّرَّتَيْنِ بِمَا صَبَرُوا۟ وَيَدْرَءُونَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ ٱلسَّيِّئَةَ وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ

Those will be given their reward twice. Why twice? Because they were sincere believers in their previous revelation, following it to the best of their ability despite its corruption. They get reward for that faithfulness. Then they recognized truth when it came in the form of Quran and embraced it. They get reward for that as well.

بِمَا صَبَرُوا۟ (for what they patiently endured) – This could refer to the patience they showed before, remaining committed to whatever truth remained in their scriptures. Or it could refer to the historical context: some believers from Abyssinia traveled to meet the Prophet ﷺ, and when they arrived, the Makkans mocked them mercilessly. "You had a book, you had education, you had a kingdom, and you're going to accept this? How stupid are you?" They had to endure ridicule for their faith.

وَيَدْرَءُونَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ ٱلسَّيِّئَةَ (And they avert evil with good) – When bad things happened to them, when evil words were directed at them, they responded with good deeds and good character. This is the quality of true believers. When negativity comes, they counter it with goodness.

وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ (And from what We have provided them, they spend) – Despite whatever they endured, they continued to give, to be generous, to spend in the path of Allah ﷻ.

translation: And when they hear ill speech, they turn away from it and say, "For us are our deeds, and for you are your deeds. Peace will be upon you; we seek not the ignorant
commentary:
وَإِذَا سَمِعُوا۟ ٱللَّغْوَ أَعْرَضُوا۟ عَنْهُ وَقَالُوا۟ لَنَآ أَعْمَـٰلُنَا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ لَا نَبْتَغِى ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ

When they hear اللَّغْوَ (vain, foolish talk), they turn away from it. We've seen this description before. True believers, when the ignorant address them, say سَلَام (peace) and move on. They don't engage in pointless arguments.

لَنَآ أَعْمَـٰلُنَا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ (For us are our deeds, and for you are your deeds) – We're responsible for ourselves. You're responsible for yourselves. We're not going to waste our time in your foolishness.

سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ (Peace be upon you) – This isn't the greeting of peace. It's a dismissal, a farewell. We want nothing to do with this conversation.

لَا نَبْتَغِى ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (We seek not the ignorant) – We're not in pursuit of those who want to argue from emotion and whim. We're not going to chase after الجَـٰهِلِين – those who speak without knowledge, who want conflict for its own sake.

translation: Indeed, [O Muhammad], you do not guide whom you like, but Allah guides whom He wills. And He is most knowing of the [rightly] guided
commentary:
إِنَّكَ لَا تَهْدِى مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ

This ayah came in a specific context. Allah ﷻ had just blessed the Prophet ﷺ with multiple family members accepting Islam – his daughters, his cousin Ali (RA), his beloved wife Khadijah (RA), his close friend Abu Bakr (RA). But the vast majority of his family had not accepted Islam. His uncle Abu Talib, who protected him and loved him dearly, never became Muslim. This must have weighed heavily on his heart.

إِنَّكَ لَا تَهْدِى مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ (Indeed, you do not guide whom you love) – You don't get to guide whoever you love. That's not your authority. That's not in your hands. وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ (But Allah guides whom He wills) – That decision belongs to Allah ﷻ alone.

وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ (And He is most knowing of those who are guided) – Here's the secret. Allah ﷻ guides المُهْتَدِين – those who are committed to guidance, those who want to be guided. Guidance isn't forced on anyone. It's offered to those who genuinely seek it with open hearts.

translation: And they say, "If we were to follow the guidance with you, we would be swept from our land." Have we not established for them a safe sanctuary to which are brought the fruits of all things as provision from Us? But most of them do not know
commentary:
وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِن نَّتَّبِعِ ٱلْهُدَىٰ مَعَكَ نُتَخَطَّفْ مِنْ أَرْضِنَآ ۚ أَوَلَمْ نُمَكِّن لَّهُمْ حَرَمًا ءَامِنًا يُجْبَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ ثَمَرَٰتُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ رِّزْقًا مِّن لَّدُنَّا وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

Some people would come to the Prophet ﷺ privately and say: We've heard the Quran. We're impressed. We don't doubt it. Actually, we believe you're right. But here's our problem: إِن نَّتَّبِعِ ٱلْهُدَىٰ مَعَكَ نُتَخَطَّفْ مِنْ أَرْضِنَآ (If we follow guidance with you, we'll be snatched from our land).

The word نُتَخَطَّفْ comes from خَطْف – like a bird snatching its prey. We'll become targets. We'll be picked off. Look at what happened to the Muslims – they used to be respected, they had status. But since they accepted Islam, they've become targets of persecution. We don't want that discrimination. We might even get expelled from our land.

Allah ﷻ responds by broadening their perspective: أَوَلَمْ نُمَكِّن لَّهُمْ حَرَمًا ءَامِنًا (Have We not established for them a safe sanctuary?) You're worried about protection? Who do you think has been protecting Makkah all this time? You live in a sacred land where tribes around you are constantly at war, yet you enjoy peace and safety. The best fruits from everywhere are brought to you يُجْبَىٰٓ – carefully selected and delivered.

This fulfills the dua of Ibrahim (AS). You enjoy economic prosperity and security in a region surrounded by conflict. Who provided that? رِّزْقًا مِّن لَّدُنَّا (as provision from Us, especially from Us). When Allah ﷻ wants to protect you, even armies of elephants can't touch you. You're asking about the Muslims not having protection – what about you? Who's been protecting you all along?

وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (But most of them do not know) – They don't see the bigger picture. They don't recognize that their safety has always come from Allah ﷻ, not from their own power or political maneuvering.

translation: And how many a city have We destroyed that was insolent in its [way of] living, and those are their dwellings which have not been inhabited after them except briefly. And it is We who were the inheritors
commentary:
وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍۭ بَطِرَتْ مَعِيشَتَهَا ۖ فَتِلْكَ مَسَـٰكِنُهُمْ لَمْ تُسْكَن مِّنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا ۖ وَكُنَّا نَحْنُ ٱلْوَٰرِثِينَ

How many towns have We destroyed? وَكَمْ creates this sense of countless civilizations, diverse peoples, different ethnicities. But they all shared one problem: بَطِرَتْ مَعِيشَتَهَا (who were insolent in their prosperity).

The word بَطِرَ means to squander the good life, to become arrogant because of luxury and ease. Allah ﷻ had given them مَعِيشَة – good life, prosperity. They had safety (which is what Makkah has) and they had economic prosperity (which is what Makkah has). These are the two pillars of a stable society. But they squandered it through arrogance and ingratitude.

فَتِلْكَ مَسَـٰكِنُهُمْ (And those are their dwellings) – When you Arabs travel to get those exotic fruits, you pass by ruins, don't you? You see the remnants of their homes. That's what's left of those luxurious places that squandered their opportunities.

لَمْ تُسْكَن مِّنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (Not inhabited after them except briefly) – Those homes aren't even livable anymore. They're not residential areas. They're just ruins, a testament to what happens when people take their blessings for granted.

وَكُنَّا نَحْنُ ٱلْوَٰرِثِينَ (And it is We who were the Inheritor) – At the end of the day, when everyone is gone and civilizations crumble, when the entire earth becomes ruins, there is only one Inheritor: Allah ﷻ. Everything returns to Him. All the wealth, all the power, all the land – it all goes back to the One who gave it in the first place. This is the ultimate reality that these civilizations forgot, and it led to their downfall.

translation: And never would your Lord have destroyed the cities until He had sent to their mother a messenger reciting to them Our verses. And We would not destroy the cities except while their people were wrongdoers
commentary:
وَمَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ مُهْلِكَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ حَتَّىٰ يَبْعَثَ فِىٓ أُمِّهَا رَسُولًا يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا ۚ وَمَا كُنَّا مُهْلِكِى ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ إِلَّا وَأَهْلُهَا ظَـٰلِمُونَ

This ayah establishes a profound principle of divine justice: Allah ﷻ would never destroy any town until He first sends a messenger to its central city - its أم, meaning mother town or capital. The previous ayah discussed Solomon (AS) and the Queen of Sheba, both representing capital cities and centers of civilization. This pattern continues here, showing us that Allah ﷻ doesn't simply send messengers anywhere - He targets the centers, the capital cities, because from these hubs the message spreads outward to all surrounding areas.

The messenger يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا - recites to them Our miraculous signs. Notice it doesn't just say "tells them" or "teaches them," but specifically *recites* - emphasizing the revelation, the divine communication that cannot be ignored or dismissed as mere human wisdom.

The ayah concludes with a critical caveat: وَمَا كُنَّا مُهْلِكِى ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ إِلَّا وَأَهْلُهَا ظَـٰلِمُونَ - We would never destroy towns except that their people were absolutely wrongdoers. The word ظَـٰلِمُونَ is particularly strong here. It's not just ظَالِم (a wrongdoer), but the emphatic form indicating they were bent on wrongdoing, committed to it, with no intention of changing their ways. Only when this becomes absolutely clear - when the evidence is overwhelming that they will not turn back - does divine punishment come.

translation: And whatever thing you [people] have been given - it is [only for] the enjoyment of worldly life and its adornment. And what is with Allah is better and more lasting; so will you not use reason
commentary:
وَمَآ أُوتِيتُم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَمَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَزِينَتُهَا ۚ وَمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقَىٰٓ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ

Whatever you've been given of any luxury or utility amounts to مَتَـٰعُ - the utilities and utensils of this temporary worldly life. The word متاع carries a fascinating depth. It primarily means something used, like a utensil or tool. Think of a spoon - you don't necessarily enjoy the spoon itself; you enjoy what's in it. A rake, a broom - these are utilities that serve a purpose beyond themselves.

The classical scholar Al-Asma'i famously searched for the natural usage of this word. He traveled through Bedouin villages, not asking people what it meant but waiting to hear it used in context. He finally witnessed a young girl cleaning dishes by a river with a brush when her pet goat grabbed the brush and ran away. She cried out, "أخذ الماعز متاعي!" - "The goat took my mata' away!" This captured it perfectly: something useful, a tool, not the object of desire itself.

Allah ﷻ is telling us not to get obsessed with the tools. Everything in this worldly life - particularly the glitz and glamour of capital cities, the economic centers where people see the most beauty and accumulation - all of it is just متاع. It's inferior (الدُّنْيَا also means lower, inferior) compared to what's coming.

وَمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقَىٰٓ - What Allah ﷻ has is better and longer lasting. Two crucial contrasts: First, whatever you have here, Allah ﷻ has a superior version waiting. You have a home? He has a better home. You have relationships? He has better relationships prepared. Second, everything here expires. Human beings, relationships, homes, cars, computers, nations, civilizations - all have expiration dates. But what Allah ﷻ has prepared is أَبْقَىٰٓ - longer lasting, in fact eternal.

Think about those rare perfect moments between spouses when they sit together and say, "Why can't every day be like this? If this could just last..." But ten minutes later, it vanishes. This is the fleeting nature of worldly joy. أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ - Why don't you think about this? Why don't you apply your intellect?

translation: Then is he whom We have promised a good promise which he will obtain like he for whom We provided enjoyment of worldly life [but] then he is, on the Day of Resurrection, among those presented [for punishment in Hell]
commentary:
أَفَمَن وَعَدْنَـٰهُ وَعْدًا حَسَنًا فَهُوَ لَـٰقِيهِ كَمَن مَّتَّعْنَـٰهُ مَتَـٰعَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ثُمَّ هُوَ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْضَرِينَ

Can the one We've promised a beautiful promise - and he's going to meet it, encounter it, see it face to face - be compared to the one We merely gave worldly luxuries to? Think of those moments when someone's eyes are covered, then they're shown a surprise gift and their face lights up. Allah ﷻ has made us beautiful promises, described them in detail - the waterfalls, the fruits, the seating arrangements of Paradise. We run wild with imagination: "Really? It's going to be like that?"

فَهُوَ لَـٰقِيهِ - Then he finally gets to meet it, to see it. He grabs the fruit and recites the ayah about that fruit. He sits on the couch and exclaims, "SubhanAllah, this is what He was describing!" Because right now, all we have are words. In advertising, they show you pictures, previews. But for Jannah, Allah ﷻ gives only words - His promise. That person eagerly awaiting that promise cannot be compared to the one who simply received متاع - utilities of this temporary, lower life.

Then on the Day of Resurrection, مِنَ ٱلْمُحْضَرِينَ - he's made to stand, presented, brought forward for the audit. You know how audits work - they examine every penny. The Prophet ﷺ once warned a wealthy Companion: "You're guaranteed Paradise, but you'll be among the last to enter." Why? "Because you're very well off, and you have a lot to answer for. The entire accounting must be finished before you can proceed."

translation: And [warn of] the Day He will call them and say, "Where are My 'partners' which you used to claim
commentary:
وَيَوْمَ يُنَادِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآءِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ كُنتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ

The Day when He will call them - first they're called by name, and terror grips them - then He'll say, "So, I have a question for you: Where are those partners of Mine, those other supposed divine entities who were going to save you?"

Here's something profound: In Surah Al-Ankabut, when Allah ﷻ asks the idol worshipers questions - "Who created the skies and earth? Who feeds you?" - their consistent answer is "Allah!" They believed in Allah ﷻ. So what was their shirk? They thought Allah ﷻ was too high, too powerful for direct connection. They needed intermediaries, sub-gods they had better connections with - like a small employee who can't reach the CEO but has an uncle who's a manager. That uncle can intercede, put in a good word.

الَّذِينَ كُنتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ - those you had such baseless confidence in. زَعْم means confidence without foundation, hoping desperately that something will come through when it has no basis. Every time this word appears in the Quran, it's in a negative context.

translation: Those upon whom the word will have come into effect will say, "Our Lord, these are the ones we led to error. We led them to error just as we were in error. We declare our disassociation [from them] to You. They did not used to worship us
commentary:
قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَوْلُ رَبَّنَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ أَغْوَيْنَآ أَغْوَيْنَـٰهُمْ كَمَا غَوَيْنَا ۖ تَبَرَّأْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ۖ مَا كَانُوٓا۟ إِيَّانَا يَعْبُدُونَ

Those upon whom the word has been justified - the leaders, the elite who deserved this response - they say, "Our Master, these are the ones we misled." But notice their excuse: they were themselves misleaders who manufactured the idols, established the festival days, created the entire culture of shirk. The main temples were in their capital cities. This is why Allah ﷻ targets the leaders of capitals - they architect the system.

"We misled them أَغْوَيْنَـٰهُمْ كَمَا غَوَيْنَا - just as we ourselves were misled." They're saying, "Yes, we caused the population to go astray because they saw us as intelligent, educated, elite. They assumed we must be acting on good reason. They trusted us. We didn't have to answer to anyone. We were too elite to be questioned. When Your messengers tried to question us, we dismissed them: 'Who do you think you are? You're just a human like us. Only the lowest class follows you.'"

تَبَرَّأْنَآ إِلَيْكَ - "We're cutting ourselves off now from all those we misled and all those who misled us." مَا كَانُوٓا۟ إِيَّانَا يَعْبُدُونَ - "They weren't worshiping us anyway. They weren't engaged in our worship at all."

translation: And it will be said, "Invoke your 'partners' " and they will invoke them; but they will not respond to them, and they will see the punishment. If only they had followed guidance
commentary:
وَقِيلَ ٱدْعُوا۟ شُرَكَآءَكُمْ فَدَعَوْهُمْ فَلَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَهُمْ وَرَأَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ۚ لَوْ أَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَهْتَدُونَ

First they were asked where their partners are - they can't see them. So what do you do when you can't see someone? You call them. "Call your false gods!"

فَدَعَوْهُمْ - So they called them, adding to their humiliation. فَلَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَهُمْ - They won't even begin to respond. وَرَأَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ - Then they all see the punishment. لَوْ أَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَهْتَدُونَ - If only they had been committed to guidance! How hard was it? How difficult was it really to avoid this humiliation?

translation: And [mention] the Day He will call them and say, "What did you answer the messengers
commentary:
وَيَوْمَ يُنَادِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُ مَاذَآ أَجَبْتُمُ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

The Day when He calls them again: "So those gods didn't respond to you. Let's talk about your responses. How did you respond to those messengers I sent?"

translation: But the information will be unapparent to them that Day, so they will not [be able to] ask one another
commentary:
فَعَمِيَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْأَنۢبَآءُ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَهُمْ لَا يَتَسَآءَلُونَ

The news, the events, have been blinded from them - they have no idea what terrors lie ahead. They're in complete shock about what's coming. You hear the rattling, the roar of Hellfire, you're about to be thrown in, but you've been blinded from understanding what's happening.

Naturally, when human beings face something scary but can't see it, they ask about it. Even a young child, hearing a chair drag, gets worried: "What's that sound?" But فَهُمْ لَا يَتَسَآءَلُونَ - they won't even have the ability to ask one another what's happening.

This connects directly to Surah An-Naba, which opens with عَمَّ يَتَسَآءَلُونَ - "What are they asking one another about?" The news they're questioning now, making jokes about, will be the very news they're denied access to on that Day. They won't be able to ask because the نبأ (news) they were repeatedly reminded of, warned about, they dismissed. Now it's too late.

translation: But as for one who had repented, believed, and done righteousness, it is promised by Allah that he will be among the successful
commentary:
فَأَمَّا مَن تَابَ وَءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَعَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُفْلِحِينَ

As for the one who repented, and revived his iman - as though through tawbah, someone comes to faith completely fresh, with a clean slate - وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا and acted righteously, فَعَسَىٰٓ - then hopefully, possibly, there's a chance أَن يَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُفْلِحِينَ - that he may become from those who attained success.

Why عَسَىٰٓ (possibly) and not absolute certainty? Because making tawbah and acting righteously doesn't guarantee you died in that state. You made that change, you tried to become better, but it doesn't mean you didn't fall back into old patterns. Just because you became from those people doesn't mean you're absolutely saved. You must stay vigilant, keep watch over yourself. Don't assume guaranteed salvation before Allah ﷻ.

translation: And your Lord creates what He wills and chooses; not for them was the choice. Exalted is Allah and high above what they associate with Him
commentary:
وَرَبُّكَ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ وَيَخْتَارُ ۗ مَا كَانَ لَهُمُ ٱلْخِيَرَةُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ

Your Master creates whatever He wills and chooses whatever He wills. A believer might look at this world and see the vast majority headed toward Hellfire, populations obsessed with entertainment, living worldly lives with no concern for the Hereafter. Beautiful cities, civilizations, communities - all heedless. A person can fall into depression: "Why create so many if only a few will have guidance?"

If we have that question, imagine how much more a messenger has it - the one whose actual job is to worry about humanity. He looks at populations and wonders, "How will this work?" Allah ﷻ responds: Your Master creates whatever He wants. He's the Master, the final authority. You don't get to question what He creates. وَيَخْتَارُ - And He chooses out of goodness whatever He wants.

مَا كَانَ لَهُمُ ٱلْخِيَرَةُ - They have no choice at all. الْخِيَرَةُ means good choice, the ability to make sound decisions. This ability isn't innate in you - it's something Allah ﷻ empowered you with. سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ - How perfect He is, how far above, وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ - and how high above the kinds of shirk they commit.

This ayah addresses divine will while maintaining human responsibility. Yes, Allah ﷻ has absolute will, but understand this correctly: Allah ﷻ chose all of humanity. How? He chose Adam (AS), and in choosing Adam (AS), He chose his entire offspring - you're from a chosen family. Even the existence of human beings is Allah's special choice. When angels questioned creating humans who might spill blood, Allah ﷻ defended us: إِنِّي أَعْلَمُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ - "I know what you don't know." We are chosen.

But being chosen doesn't guarantee you'll fulfill your responsibility. Just like someone hired for a job - being chosen for the position doesn't mean you'll perform well. Similarly, in the previous ayah, making tawbah and doing good deeds doesn't guarantee you'll continue that path. You can mess up anywhere along the way.

Allah ﷻ is saying: I chose to give you revelation, to send you a messenger, to grant you access to wisdom - you didn't pick that. But you can squander it. You can refuse to take advantage of it.

translation: And your Lord knows what their breasts conceal and what they declare
commentary:
وَرَبُّكَ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُكِنُّ صُدُورُهُمْ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ

Your Master knows what they keep deeply vaulted, hidden inside their chests, and what they expose publicly.

translation: And He is Allah; there is no deity except Him. To Him is [due all] praise in the first [life] and the Hereafter. And His is the [final] decision, and to Him you will be returned
commentary:
وَهُوَ ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْحَمْدُ فِى ٱلْأُولَىٰ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَلَهُ ٱلْحُكْمُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ

He is Allah ﷻ - there is no deity worthy of worship except Him. لَهُ ٱلْحَمْدُ فِى ٱلْأُولَىٰ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ - To Him alone belongs all praise in the beginning and at the end. Whether at the beginning of the Prophet's ﷺ struggle when the House of Allah ﷻ was filled with idols, or at the end when the idols were destroyed and the House was purified - all praise belongs to Him alone in both states.

Also: in this life (الْأُولَىٰ), no matter how much people disbelieve or blaspheme, it doesn't diminish Allah's glory. Hamd still belongs to Him. And in the Hereafter (الْـَٔاخِرَةِ), when everything becomes clear, praise obviously belongs to Him. What power do we have anyway? We're like ants. What can we truly change of Allah's stature?

وَلَهُ ٱلْحُكْمُ - To Him alone belongs all wisdom and all rule. وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ - And to Him alone you will be returned. You have a court date. Some people try to flee court dates, but warrants are issued. Judgment Day is a court date you cannot miss. You don't have a choice - you're already under arrest, and you will be taken.

## Ayahs 71-72

قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ سَرْمَدًا إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَنْ إِلَـٰهٌ غَيْرُ ٱللَّهِ يَأْتِيكُم بِضِيَآءٍ ۖ أَفَلَا تَسْمَعُونَ

قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلنَّهَارَ سَرْمَدًا إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَنْ إِلَـٰهٌ غَيْرُ ٱللَّهِ يَأْتِيكُم بِلَيْلٍ تَسْكُنُونَ فِيهِ ۖ أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ

Tell them: Consider if Allah ﷻ made night سَرْمَدًا - permanent, endless, immobile, unchanging - over you until the Day of Resurrection. What god other than Allah ﷻ could bring you any warm light? أَفَلَا تَسْمَعُونَ - Why aren't you listening?

And if Allah ﷻ made day permanent over you until Resurrection - what god besides Allah ﷻ could bring you night تَسْكُنُونَ فِيهِ - in which you find tranquility, in which you can calm down, take a break? أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ - Why don't you then see?

These ayahs aren't just about nature's power. They address something deeper: the necessity of change. If night remained permanent, warmth and light would disappear - things would freeze, life would stop. If day remained permanent, you'd become so active, so exhausted from constant activity, you'd work yourself to death. Change is critical.

This applies at the planetary level but also personally. You need breaks. The work of Deen is important, but it's not 24/7. The Prophet ﷺ told his Companion who wanted to pray all night: "Pray most of the night, except a little - you need rest." He taught us to fast Sunnah days but not every single day. Have a spiritual life, but also get married, enjoy good things, wear nice perfume. Recognize that Jannah is better, but enjoy this life too within limits. You can't go all-in on one dimension.

This principle extends to Islamic movements and struggles. Every movement for Islam starts with enthusiasm - people inspired to bring light to the world, motivated by the messenger's promise and Allah's words. They keep struggling regardless of immediate results because they see the big picture. But when movements see some success or continue for a long time, motivation gets rusty. What started as "let's give light to the world" slowly becomes "we own the light and they don't" - an elitist mentality, an us-versus-them attitude.

That's the first sign you need a break, a reset. If you continue this way, deterioration accelerates. The next stage: you start breaking apart internally, fragmenting over who really has the right understanding, who's the legitimate movement. What began as a cause to guide the world becomes infighting over who owns Allah's guidance.

This pattern has repeated throughout history - even after the Prophet ﷺ and the unified Sahaba (RA). One generation, two generations, then naturally infighting emerged. Bani Israel experienced it too. If this happens even with what the prophets established, we should expect and prepare for this reality in every generation.

The key? Go back to basics. Examine movements carefully: What struggle do I want to support? What effort was once great but has deteriorated? We don't have loyalties to labels or organizations - our loyalty is to the cause. Allah ﷻ may grant success to one group temporarily, then shift it elsewhere. Keep your eyes open: What's doing genuine good now? Support that. But tomorrow if it's no longer on track, your loyalty shouldn't be to the label but to the cause itself.

translation: Say, "Have you considered: if Allah should make for you the night continuous until the Day of Resurrection, what deity other than Allah could bring you light? Then will you not hear

Say, "Have you considered: if Allah should make for you the day continuous until the Day of Resurrection, what deity other than Allah could bring you a night in which you may rest? Then will you not see
And out of His mercy He made for you the night and the day that you may rest therein and [by day] seek from His bounty and [that] perhaps you will be grateful
commentary:
وَمِن رَّحْمَتِهِۦ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ لِتَسْكُنُوا۟ فِيهِ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ

From His mercy, He made for you the night and the day - لِتَسْكُنُوا۟ فِيهِ so you can find rest in it, وَلِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ and so you can pursue from His favor, وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ and so that you all may be grateful.

translation: And [warn of] the Day He will call them and say, "Where are my 'partners' which you used to claim
commentary:
وَيَوْمَ يُنَادِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآءِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ كُنتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ

The Day when He calls them again: "Where are My partners about whom you had such baseless confidence?"

translation: And We will extract from every nation a witness and say, "Produce your proof," and they will know that the truth belongs to Allah, and lost from them is that which they used to invent
commentary:
وَنَزَعْنَا مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيدًا فَقُلْنَا هَاتُوا۟ بُرْهَـٰنَكُمْ فَعَلِمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱلْحَقَّ لِلَّهِ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ

We will pull out, yank out from every nation a witness - the elite whom Allah ﷻ extracts. Some scholars say these are the prophets who will testify against their people. Allah ﷻ will speak to the people: "Bring your evidence!" 

"I brought My evidence - I delivered the message. Here's My witness. You recognize him? Remember all the time he spent with you? All those jokes you made against him? Now show your proof for why you didn't believe him, why you didn't take him seriously."

فَعَلِمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱلْحَقَّ لِلَّهِ - Then they come to know that truth belongs to Allah ﷻ. وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ - And everything they used to fabricate is lost, disappeared. Those false gods they were calling? They can't find them anywhere.

translation: Indeed, Qarun was from the people of Moses, but he tyrannized them. And We gave him of treasures whose keys would burden a band of strong men; thereupon his people said to him, "Do not exult. Indeed, Allah does not like the exultant
commentary:
۞ إِنَّ قَـٰرُونَ كَانَ مِن قَوْمِ مُوسَىٰ فَبَغَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلْكُنُوزِ مَآ إِنَّ مَفَاتِحَهُۥ لَتَنُوٓأُ بِٱلْعُصْبَةِ أُو۟لِى ٱلْقُوَّةِ إِذْ قَالَ لَهُۥ قَوْمُهُۥ لَا تَفْرَحْ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْفَرِحِينَ

Now we reach the end of the surah, and the story comes full circle. Multiple times in the Quran, Musa (AS) begins and ends passages, his character bookending major themes. Sometimes this happens even between surahs. Here at the conclusion, the Quran brings us back to Musa (AS), but this time focusing on an internal enemy rather than an external one like Fir'awn.

إِنَّ قَـٰرُونَ كَانَ مِن قَوْمِ مُوسَىٰ - Indeed, Qarun was from the nation of Musa (AS). Why emphasize this obvious fact? Because if this were after crossing the sea, everyone in the desert would obviously be from Musa's nation - no need to state it. But this story takes place in Egypt, and it highlights a critical reality: there are two kinds of enemies - external and internal. The internal enemy can be just as dangerous, if not more harmful, than the external one.

Why would there be any doubt Qarun was from Musa's people? Because of his extreme wealth. How could any member of the enslaved race be this wealthy? Remember from the beginning of this surah: when a king weakens people, he creates a class society. He takes the slave population and finds sellouts among them, gives them wealth and positions, uses them to monitor the slaves, ensure their loyalty, and corrupt their mentality so they don't think independently of the regime.

The colonizers perfected this strategy - they gave positions to sellouts, dressed them in uniforms, provided homes, education for their children. The rest of the enslaved population would look at them with envy: "We're living like this while he's living like that. His kids go to schools, learn the colonizer's language. I wish I could be like him." This creates division, an elite class within the oppressed community itself.

But there's also a social dimension beyond politics. Within Muslim communities - even before Islam in Musa's time - you have corrupt wealthy people who go out of their way to display their wealth. We see versions of this today: the class divisions within Muslim communities between those struggling in modest circumstances and the wealthy elite.

Consider the immigrant experience in places like New York - families living in 500 square foot apartments (your typical one-bedroom in New York is the size of a closet in Texas), everyone working multiple jobs, saving every penny. Then they get invited to a wedding on Long Island at an expensive catering hall. They drive their beat-up car with pizza delivery stickers, park next to luxury vehicles, see the giant gate, people in expensive clothes, mountains of gifts. Their eyes glaze over: "One day, insha'Allah..."

They visit the home for a walima, see the front porch, the backyard, count the rooms. They can't stop talking about it because this comparison consumes them. This wishing becomes a disease. The "haves" should be the most giving in society - Allah ﷻ didn't give them wealth just to show off. In a disbelieving society, they have entire TV shows dedicated to showing off - house tours, car shows. But even in Muslim society, this can happen.

Qarun represents this archetype that appears in every community, creating problems just by existing. فَبَغَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ - He rebelled against them, became a traitor. Though from them, he wasn't for them - he was against them, his loyalties corrupted.

وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلْكُنُوزِ - We had granted him treasures. He thought Fir'awn was hooking him up, but Allah ﷻ says "We granted" - acknowledging that even this was a test from Allah ﷻ. If Allah ﷻ wanted, He could have stopped this earlier. مِنَ ٱلْكُنُوزِ - treasures, plural. Not one vault but multiple vaults, like having accounts at different banks, except these were physical security deposits containing actual wealth, not virtual money.

مَآ إِنَّ مَفَاتِحَهُۥ لَتَنُوٓأُ بِٱلْعُصْبَةِ أُو۟لِى ٱلْقُوَّةِ - The keys to his treasures would weigh down a strong band of people. تَنُوٓأُ means to lift something with exhaustion. He hired groups of muscular men - imagine bodybuilders, football players - and even they struggled to lift just the keys to his vaults.

These weren't modern lock-and-key systems. Ancient security for massive gates required enormous logs of wood hung as locks - tree trunks essentially - so heavy that even 20 men couldn't easily steal them. You needed extraordinarily powerful people (عُصْبَة أُو۟لِى ٱلْقُوَّةِ) who would still struggle (تَنُوٓأُ) just to lift these "keys" and seal the vault. When Yusuf's (AS) brothers wanted to show their strength, they used the word عُصْبَة.

Think about this: he had to hire several strong men just to manage the keys. What does that tell you about the treasures inside? This man possessed extraordinary wealth - so much that he needed multiple employees just for key management.

إِذْ قَالَ لَهُۥ قَوْمُهُۥ - When his people said to him - meaning the sincere believers among Musa's nation who tried to advise him - لَا تَفْرَحْ - "Don't be overly happy." Don't go around publicly showing off, "Yeah, life is good, pretty nice," riding through town with expensive horses and carriages, deliberately going through poorer neighborhoods, riding slowly so people notice, rolling down his window: "Hey, how's it going? Just riding around, you know..."

You see this even today - teenagers outside high schools parking cars with spinner rims, blasting music, giant "Honda" letters on their Toyota Celica windshield, showing off. But here's the thing: Qarun couldn't show off to the Egyptians because to them he was still a dog, worthless, a sellout slave eating their scraps, without respect. So who could he show off to? He went back to his own people, the ones he could look down upon.

This mirrors situations throughout history - like Arabs connected to the Roman Empire who had wealth and businesses but would lower their eyes when Roman soldiers passed. On one hand, such show-offs toward their own people; on the other, complete cowards before their masters.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْفَرِحِينَ - Indeed, Allah ﷻ does not love those who show off, who go overboard, who are excessively pleased with the wealth they have.

translation: But seek, through that which Allah has given you, the home of the Hereafter; and [yet], do not forget your share of the world. And do good as Allah has done good to you. And desire not corruption in the land. Indeed, Allah does not like corrupters
commentary:
وَٱبْتَغِ فِيمَآ ءَاتَىٰكَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلدَّارَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ ۖ وَلَا تَنسَ نَصِيبَكَ مِنَ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَأَحْسِن كَمَآ أَحْسَنَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَيْكَ ۖ وَلَا تَبْغِ ٱلْفَسَادَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ

The sincere believers offer Qarun comprehensive advice, which serves as guidance for anyone with wealth in any Muslim community:

**First**: وَٱبْتَغِ فِيمَآ ءَاتَىٰكَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلدَّارَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ - "In whatever Allah ﷻ has given you, pursue the home of the Hereafter." Your priority should be Akhirah. Someone struggling to find their next meal, understandably obsessed with lunch - that's excusable. But you have enough to feed yourself and two generations down. You shouldn't be obsessed with dunya anymore. Allah ﷻ has freed you from worldly concerns, so now your concern should be Akhirah.

This is why we want financial independence - to serve Allah ﷻ better. But what happens when wealth comes? Wants transform into needs. "We need a swimming pool. We need to remodel. We need another car." Things that were wants before slowly become "necessities."

Right now, Musa (AS) has made the call to Islam. He needs financing to spread this message across Egyptian lands, to deliver it far and wide. Qarun has the financial means to support this cause, to invest in Akhirah. Yet he's vaulting it away, hoarding it.

**Second**: وَلَا تَنسَ نَصِيبَكَ مِنَ ٱلدُّنْيَا - "Don't forget your portion from this world." We're not saying become a monk who abandons everything, gives up your Mercedes for a bicycle. No. Take care of yourself reasonably, but there are limits. Also, don't forget your responsibilities in worldly life - your portion includes your family, friends, close relatives. They're part of your responsibility too.

**Third**: وَأَحْسِن كَمَآ أَحْسَنَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَيْكَ - "Excel just as Allah ﷻ has excelled toward you." You're a role model. People look up to you - you're wealthy, well-educated, knowledgeable, well-dressed. When people see you, they should speak of good things you do. They should say, "He must be good because he does good things, and Allah ﷻ blessed him. So I should try doing good things too." You should encourage good behavior just by your example.

Another meaning: أَحْسِن means be good to people. The Arabic saying goes: "People are slaves to goodness" - when you're good to them, they're yours (not as servants, but they love you). So be genuinely good to people. Why is it that the only time you talk to us is when you're showing off or telling us how to comply with the king, or if you know what's good for us? Why are you such a traitor?

**Fourth**: وَلَا تَبْغِ ٱلْفَسَادَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - "Do not pursue corruption in the land." When you have money, you have ability you didn't have before. Now you can buy concert tickets, VIP passes, expensive liquor, drugs. All of this is funded by wealthy people. If the wealthy weren't going to clubs, buying expensive wines, doing drugs, purchasing VIP passes, that entire industry would die. It lives off people with what Americans call "disposable income."

Allah ﷻ is telling the wealthy: Don't create more corruption. "But I'm just buying concert tickets, I'm not causing corruption!" Yes, you are - the entire industry rests on you as the consumer. You're creating and sustaining it.

Another dimension: Just by not doing what has been advised - not prioritizing Akhirah, not excelling, not being good to people - just by sitting there breathing, you're creating corruption in the land. Your mere existence in that state, your example, is pursuit of corruption.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ - Indeed, Allah ﷻ does not love those who cause corruption.

translation: He said, "I was only given it because of knowledge I have." Did he not know that Allah had destroyed before him of generations those who were greater than him in power and greater in accumulation [of wealth]? But the criminals, about their sins, will not be asked
commentary:
قَالَ إِنَّمَآ أُوتِيتُهُۥ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ عِندِىٓ ۚ أَوَلَمْ يَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَدْ أَهْلَكَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ مَنْ هُوَ أَشَدُّ مِنْهُ قُوَّةً وَأَكْثَرُ جَمْعًا ۚ وَلَا يُسْـَٔلُ عَن ذُنُوبِهِمُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ

Qarun responds to those calling him to faith with a statement that reveals the depths of his arrogance: إِنَّمَآ أُوتِيتُهُۥ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ عِندِىٓ - "I have only been given all of this based on knowledge that I possess." This is a critical moment in understanding the disease of the heart that materialism creates.

Notice the grammatical structure Qarun uses. When you say "I have something," you claim ownership. But when you say "I have been given something," you acknowledge that someone else gave it to you. The passive voice أُوتِيتُ ("I have been given") implies credit belongs elsewhere. So whom is Qarun crediting?

There are two possibilities, both deeply problematic. The first is that he's acknowledging Allah ﷻ as the source, but claiming he earned it through his own talent and intelligence. This mindset exists widely today: "Yes, Allah ﷻ blessed me, but that's because I'm special. I deserve it because of my talents." In Qarun's case, it's his exceptional intelligence that he believes made him worthy of such wealth.

The second interpretation is that Firaun chose him from among the people, granting him these privileges based on knowledge Qarun possessed. Perhaps it was his intelligence, his understanding of how to manage affairs, or even his knowledge of the people's activities that he reported back to the regime. He might be saying, "I made the right investments, put my money in the right ventures at the right time, and that's how I accumulated this wealth."

The critical insight here is that even when someone full of themselves admits they didn't earn something on their own, they still find a way to claim credit. أُوتِيتُهُۥ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ عِندِىٓ - "I've been given this based on knowledge I possess." It's arrogance wearing the disguise of humility. One person says outright, "This is mine, not from Allah ﷻ." Another says, "It is from Allah ﷻ, but only because I deserve it." Both are forms of arrogance.

We cannot claim to deserve what Allah ﷻ gives us. This applies directly to our lives today. You got a job? "I got it because I'm really qualified. I did well on the interview." That's not the complete truth. Yes, you should have qualifications. You should prepare well for interviews. But your effort is not the reason something happens. Your effort is what Allah ﷻ requires of you, but results come from Him alone. Allah ﷻ gives results to those who make effort, but the effort itself is never the cause of success.

Allah ﷻ responds to this attitude with severe words: أَوَلَمْ يَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَدْ أَهْلَكَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ - "Didn't he know that Allah ﷻ had already destroyed many nations before him?" The divine anger in this response is palpable. This attitude of self-sufficiency brings about the rage of Allah ﷻ. He reminds Qarun that entire civilizations have been wiped out, people مَنْ هُوَ أَشَدُّ مِنْهُ قُوَّةً وَأَكْثَرُ جَمْعًا - who were far more powerful than him, who had greater wealth and larger armies.

وَلَا يُسْـَٔلُ عَن ذُنُوبِهِمُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ - "And the criminals aren't even asked about their crimes." When Allah's ﷻ judgment comes, there's no need for interrogation. He already knows what the crimes are. The criminals don't have to be sat down and taken through an entire process for Allah ﷻ to know what they've done.

translation: So he came out before his people in his adornment. Those who desired the worldly life said, "Oh, would that we had like what was given to Qarun. Indeed, he is one of great fortune
commentary:
فَخَرَجَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ فِى زِينَتِهِۦ ۖ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُرِيدُونَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا يَـٰلَيْتَ لَنَا مِثْلَ مَآ أُوتِىَ قَـٰرُونُ إِنَّهُۥ لَذُو حَظٍّ عَظِيمٍ

فَخَرَجَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ فِى زِينَتِهِۦ - "So he came out before his people in his adornment." Picture Qarun emerging from his estate, decked out in his finest clothes, servants accompanying him, riding in his impressive carriage. The display is deliberate, meant to impress and overwhelm.

قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُرِيدُونَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا - "Those who wanted the worldly life said..." This description is revealing. Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn't describe them as "those who have worldly life" but "those who want worldly life." We're learning something fundamental here: materialism is not about having wealth. Materialism is about having wealth in your heart.

You could possess tremendous wealth in your hands and not be a materialist. You could have nothing in your hands and be completely materialistic. These people looking at Qarun were poor, yet they were the materialists because their hearts were consumed with wanting what he had.

يَـٰلَيْتَ لَنَا مِثْلَ مَآ أُوتِىَ قَـٰرُونُ - "Oh, if only we had something like what Qarun has been given!" This is the voice of jealousy and misplaced desire. "Man, it would be so awesome if I had that car. Can you imagine living in a house like that? I'd take the upstairs room. Which one would you want? No, I'd prefer the side room." The entire fantasy plays out in their minds, this imagining of possessing what someone else has.

إِنَّهُۥ لَذُو حَظٍّ عَظِيمٍ - "He is truly the possessor of great fortune." The word حَظّ means luck or fortune. In old English, we might translate this simply as: "He's so lucky!" That's the depth of their analysis. They see his wealth as pure good fortune, not recognizing the spiritual poverty that comes with such arrogance.

translation: But those who had been given knowledge said, "Woe to you! The reward of Allah is better for he who believes and does righteousness. And none are granted it except the patient
commentary:
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ وَيْلَكُمْ ثَوَابُ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ لِّمَنْ ءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا وَلَا يُلَقَّىٰهَآ إِلَّا ٱلصَّـٰبِرُونَ

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ - "And those who had been given knowledge said..." Now we hear from a different group, those with real knowledge, with understanding.

وَيْلَكُمْ - "Woe to you!" or more colloquially, "What's wrong with you people? Get your act together! Wake up!" This is a sharp rebuke, a wake-up call to those mesmerized by material display.

ثَوَابُ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ - "The reward of Allah ﷻ is better." The compensation, the payment for good deeds that comes from Allah ﷻ is far superior to anything in this world. لِّمَنْ ءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا - "For whoever believes and does righteous deeds."

This is where we see the Quran's pedagogical brilliance. Either the Quran mentions a story and then extracts the lesson, or it presents a lesson and then illustrates it through a story. The story is never the point in itself; it always supports a thesis. Earlier in this surah, we were told: مَآ عِندَكُمْ يَنفَدُ ۖ وَمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ بَاقٍ - "Whatever you have will perish, but what Allah ﷻ has is better and more lasting." That theoretical principle is now being presented to us as a real historical conversation. People actually lived this. They actually had to choose between being dazzled by Qarun's wealth or holding firm to what Allah ﷻ promises.

وَلَا يُلَقَّىٰهَآ إِلَّا ٱلصَّـٰبِرُونَ - "And none will attain it except those who are patient," except those who have sabr, self-control. Only those who can control themselves, who can resist the pull of materialism and jealousy, will come into contact with that real treasure.

If you truly want to desire something, desire Jannah. Don't be jealous of what people have in this world. Allah ﷻ tells us elsewhere: وَلَا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلَىٰ مَا مَتَّعْنَا بِهِۦٓ أَزْوَٰجًا مِّنْهُمْ - "Don't extend your eyes toward what We have given some of them to enjoy." Don't live a life of jealousy, constantly wishing for what others have. Don't compare yourself endlessly: "My sister, my parents love her more. She's smarter, she's prettier, she's better at everything." Or with wealth and status: "If only I had what they have, then I'd be happy."

The truth is, Allah ﷻ has favored each person in different ways. He gave some people wealth, others health, others height, good looks, citizenship, admission to prestigious schools. He favored some with things He didn't give you, and He gave you things He didn't give them. Every single person has been given preference in some area. Don't wish for what someone else has. Those big, luxurious homes? They also have fights and problems within their walls.

translation: And We caused the earth to swallow him and his home. And there was for him no company to aid him other than Allah, nor was he of those who [could] defend themselves
commentary:
فَخَسَفْنَا بِهِۦ وَبِدَارِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ فَمَا كَانَ لَهُۥ مِن فِئَةٍ يَنصُرُونَهُۥ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنتَصِرِينَ

فَخَسَفْنَا بِهِۦ وَبِدَارِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ - "Then We caused the earth to swallow him and his home." The verb خَسَفَ means to sink into the soil, to cave in. It's as though a targeted earthquake came and collapsed the foundations of his house while he was enjoying his mansion. The entire structure began sinking into the ground, وَبِدَارِهِ - along with his home, everything he had built.

Some narrations mention that the earth swallowed everything except his head, which remained visible so people could witness what happens to those whose hearts are consumed by materialism. Again, understand this clearly: he wasn't destroyed because he was wealthy. He was destroyed because he was arrogant, because he loved his wealth more than his Creator, because showing off and elevating himself became his aspiration.

فَمَا كَانَ لَهُۥ مِن فِئَةٍ يَنصُرُونَهُۥ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ - "He had no group to help him against Allah ﷻ." The word فِئَة refers to a large group or army. He couldn't even find a large group to aid him, let alone defend him against Allah's ﷻ decree. وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنتَصِرِينَ - "And he could not even avenge himself." He couldn't help himself, couldn't save himself from his fate.

translation: And those who had wished for his position the previous day began to say, "Oh, how Allah extends provision to whom He wills of His servants and restricts it! If not that Allah had conferred favor on us, He would have caused it to swallow us. Oh, how the disbelievers do not succeed
commentary:
وَأَصْبَحَ ٱلَّذِينَ تَمَنَّوْا۟ مَكَانَهُۥ بِٱلْأَمْسِ يَقُولُونَ وَيْكَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ وَيَقْدِرُ ۖ لَوْلَآ أَن مَّنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْنَا لَخَسَفَ بِنَا ۖ وَيْكَأَنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ

وَأَصْبَحَ ٱلَّذِينَ تَمَنَّوْا۟ مَكَانَهُۥ بِٱلْأَمْسِ يَقُولُونَ - "And those who had wished for his position the day before began saying..." The very people who were fantasizing about living his life, who were saying "if only we had what he has," suddenly had their eyes opened.

وَيْكَأَنَّ - This expression وَيْكَ is an exclamation of shock and realization: "Oh my goodness! Wake up!" It's the sound of sudden understanding dawning.

ٱللَّهَ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ وَيَقْدِرُ - "Allah ﷻ expands provision for whomever He wills of His servants, and He restricts it." The verb بَسَطَ means to expand, to spread out without calculation or limit. When Allah ﷻ expands someone's provision, He's saying: "Take it, enjoy it, enjoy it, enjoy it." No calculations, no restrictions.

But وَيَقْدِرُ - He also "restricts" or "measures out." The verb قَدَرَ means to calculate precisely, to budget. When you budget, you make calculations: this much for rent, this much for utilities, this much for food. When Allah ﷻ sets a qadr, a calculation for you, it means your provision has been limited, measured out carefully.

لَوْلَآ أَن مَّنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْنَا لَخَسَفَ بِنَا - "Had it not been that Allah ﷻ favored us, He would have caused us to be swallowed up as well!" Their realization is profound: "We were thinking just like him. We had materialism in our hearts just as he did. He wasn't destroyed because he was wealthy; he was destroyed because he was a show-off, because he loved his wealth, because that's what he aspired to. And we were following the same path. If Allah ﷻ hadn't done us this tremendous favor, we would have been buried along with him."

وَيْكَأَنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ - "Oh! The disbelievers will never succeed!" Another cry of realization. Success, true falah, will never come to those who reject faith.

translation: That home of the Hereafter We assign to those who do not desire exaltedness upon the earth or corruption. And the [best] outcome is for the righteous
commentary:
تِلْكَ ٱلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ نَجْعَلُهَا لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُرِيدُونَ عُلُوًّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فَسَادًا ۚ وَٱلْعَـٰقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ

تِلْكَ ٱلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ - "That home of the Hereafter." The real home, the permanent residence. This home of Qarun just collapsed, fell apart, was swallowed by the earth. But there's another home, a lasting one.

نَجْعَلُهَا لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُرِيدُونَ عُلُوًّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - "We will grant it to those who do not desire exaltedness in the land." The word عُلُوّ means elevation, height, superiority over others. These are people who don't seek to be elevated above others, who don't aspire for material superiority to show off or lord it over people.

This doesn't mean a person can't be successful, can't get a promotion, can't become a CEO, can't build a thriving business. All of that is fine. The question is: what's the intention? Are you doing it to show off? To prove you're better than others? Or are you simply doing your best work with what Allah ﷻ has given you?

وَلَا فَسَادًا - "And do not desire corruption." The word فَسَاد encompasses all forms of corruption and decay. Consider how corruption operates in the real world. Take Las Vegas, one of the most corrupt places in modern society. What keeps it afloat? Not the dealers at the blackjack tables, not the cab drivers, not the security guards. They're just employees. It's kept alive by people with what modern economics calls "disposable income." People with money to spare who buy the tickets, book the hotels, gamble away their wealth. The corruption survives because of those who want it, who participate in it, who fund it.

I once flew to Las Vegas for a conference. The plane landed, and I looked out the window to see a private jet with Arabic script on the side, an international flight from somewhere in the Gulf. I thought, "I want to give them the benefit of the doubt, but this is hard to witness." Wealth has a responsibility, and when it's used to support fasad, it becomes a means of spiritual destruction.

I have a close friend who's a doctor in that city, extremely successful, involved with multiple hospitals simultaneously because his services are so valuable. Yet he lives a remarkably modest life. All his peers who work under him have enormous mansions, but he lives like any regular person. When people visit his home, they're confused. "Is this your brother's house? Are you moving?" No, he's not moving. They're just shocked that someone in his position has such a normal home. It's not a bad house; it's a nice, comfortable home. It's just not a 45-bedroom mansion with artificial waterfalls in the backyard.

Why does he live this way? Because he doesn't want 'uluw, he doesn't want to elevate himself above others. And you can do so much good with wealth when it's not tied up in showing off. You're not missing out on anything when you forego those luxuries. At the end of the day, every luxury of this world comes from the same earth we do, and it will return to it.

وَٱلْعَـٰقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ - "And the final outcome belongs to those who have taqwa," those who are conscious of Allah ﷻ, who guard themselves from His displeasure.

translation: Whoever comes [on the Day of Judgement] with a good deed will have better than it; and whoever comes with an evil deed - then those who did evil deeds will not be recompensed except [as much as] what they used to do
commentary:
مَن جَآءَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُۥ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهَا ۖ وَمَن جَآءَ بِٱلسَّيِّئَةِ فَلَا يُجْزَى ٱلَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ إِلَّا مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

مَن جَآءَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُۥ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهَا - "Whoever comes with a good deed will have better than it." Notice the wording: not equal to it, but خَيْرٌ - better than it. A single good deed, done with sincerity, brings rewards that far exceed the deed itself. This is the mercy and generosity of Allah ﷻ.

وَمَن جَآءَ بِٱلسَّيِّئَةِ فَلَا يُجْزَى ٱلَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ إِلَّا مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ - "And whoever comes with an evil deed, then those who did evil deeds will not be recompensed except with the equivalent of what they used to do." For evil, there's justice: you get exactly what you earned, no more. But for good, there's boundless mercy: you get far more than you earned.

As we approach the conclusion of this surah, notice how we're circling back to the theme of materialism. The surah began with إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ عَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - "Indeed, Firaun exalted himself in the land." And near the end, we have تِلْكَ ٱلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ نَجْعَلُهَا لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُرِيدُونَ عُلُوًّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - "That home of the Hereafter, We grant it to those who do not desire exaltedness in the land." The bookends of the surah mirror each other, warning against the desire for superiority and material elevation.

translation: Indeed, [O Muhammad], He who imposed upon you the Qur'an will take you back to a place of return. Say, "My Lord is most knowing of who brings guidance and who is in clear error
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِى فَرَضَ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لَرَآدُّكَ إِلَىٰ مَعَادٍ ۚ قُل رَّبِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ مَن جَآءَ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَمَنْ هُوَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِى فَرَضَ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لَرَآدُّكَ إِلَىٰ مَعَادٍ - "Indeed, the One who imposed upon you the Quran will surely return you to a place of return." The word فَرَضَ means to mandate, to make obligatory. Allah ﷻ, who made the Quran mandatory upon you, who gave you this tremendous responsibility, will bring you back.

This ayah was revealed as the Prophet ﷺ was leaving Makkah during the migration. His eyes kept looking back at his beloved hometown, the place of his birth, where he grew up, where he received the first revelation. And Allah ﷻ reveals this reassurance: لَرَآدُّكَ - "I will surely return you." Don't worry.
